PREFACE .
This editionof the Codes contains allamendments ,and notes of decisions
to January, 1895, includingcases reported in 95 Ky . and 16 Ky . Law
Reporter. Unreporteddecisionsof the Court of Appeals found inthe Ky .
Law Reportersbearingupon sectionsof the Codes have alsobeen inserted.
An index in the middle of a book seems out of place, and I have put
indexestoboth Codes, as wellas forms, at the end of the volume.
The catchwords tosections, the indexesand the noteshave been revised,
rewrittenand made more complete,and many forms not in former editions
have been added .
In an appendix willbe found the sectionsof the Kentucky Statutes
relating:
1. To the jurisdictionof allcourts in the State.
2. To courtshaving fourjudges.
3. To courtshaving continuoussessions.
4. Selectionof specialjudges.
5 . Changes of venue in civiland criminal cases.
Also the laws of theUnitedStatesrelatingtotheRemoval of Actionsand
Prosecutionstothe United StatesCourts,and theRules adopted by theCourt
of Appeals of Kentucky .
In the citationof cases I have not assumed to criticiseor comment ,
nor undertaken to say what the Court ofAppeals willdecidewhen itcomes
to construesectionsnot heretoforepassedupon , but have contentedmyself
with statingthe law as I have found itin the cases. My observationhas
been thatthe courtdoes not always adopt the viewsof the commentator,and
thatexpressionsof opinionare more misleadingthan useful.
The Kentucky Statutescontainsthe generallaws of the Statenot found
in the Codes, and the relationbetween the Statutesand Codes isso intimate
that I have in every instancewhere eithera sectionof the Codes or a
note theretocontainedany referenceto the Statutes,or any sectionof the
Statuteshad any bearing on the Codes, cited and noted the sectionof
the Statutesapplicable.
NEW CASTLE ,KY ., March , 1895.
JOHN D. CARROLL .
589078

classification.title: Civil Code of Practice
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code of Practice defines civil cases, civil actions, and special proceedings. It clarifies that civil cases are either actions or special proceedings, and a civil action is a demand made through pleadings in a court of justice to enforce a plaintiff's alleged right against a defendant. Special proceedings are defined as any other type of civil case.

It does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly refers to race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of practice.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines the forms of civil actions, distinguishing between ordinary and equitable actions. It details how actions are transferred between dockets and courts, and the general principles of pleading and stating a cause of action. It also provides examples of actions that fall under equitable jurisdiction, such as divorce, alimony, and partition of land, and actions that are typically ordinary, like assault and battery, debt recovery, and fraud.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 and deals with the procedural aspects of civil lawsuits, specifically the classification and transfer of actions between different court dockets (ordinary and equitable). There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895, Section 20
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines the procedures for transferring civil actions between the ordinary (law) and equity dockets. It details when a defendant can request a transfer, when the court can order a transfer without a motion, and the conditions under which a transfer might be deemed necessary due to complex questions or accounts. It also touches upon how judgments are treated when actions are heard on the wrong docket and the right to a jury trial for legal issues within equitable actions.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text deals with the procedural aspects of transferring civil cases between different court dockets (ordinary and equity). It discusses the conditions and motions for such transfers, the court's discretion in ordering them, and the implications for jury trials and judgments. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest a discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code from 1895.

It details conditions under which a plaintiff can prevent such a transfer by filing an affidavit of belief in success and risk of collection, and requires the defendant to post a bond if the transfer is granted under certain circumstances. It also addresses waiver of errors related to the form of action and the applicability of the code's provisions to all civil actions.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The statute focuses solely on legal procedure and the rights of defendants and plaintiffs within the civil court system. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: § 1919 [31] Assignee of chose in action - check or bill.
classification.summary: This section outlines the rules regarding the assignment of a chose in action (a right to bring a lawsuit). It states that an assignee's action is subject to any discounts, set-offs, or defenses that were available against the original assignor. If the assignment is not authorized by statute, the assignor must remain a party to the action. This section specifically excludes bills of exchange, promissory notes on the footing of bills of exchange, common orders, and checks from its provisions. The text also includes annotations and case law related to various aspects of assignments and legal actions.

The category is null as it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: PARTIES TO ACTIONS GENERALLY
classification.summary: This section of the 1895 Civil and Criminal Code outlines general rules for parties involved in legal actions. It specifies that persons with a shared interest in the subject of an action and in obtaining the demanded relief may be joined as plaintiffs, unless otherwise provided. It also states that any person claiming an adverse interest or necessary for a complete determination of the question can be made a defendant. Furthermore, parties united in interest must be joined as either plaintiffs or defendants.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details general rules of civil procedure regarding the joinder of parties in legal actions. It does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly refers to race, segregation, or any other characteristic that would classify it as a Jim Crow law. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for is_jim_crow, with a confidence of 1.0, and the category is null. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code. The racial indicator is 'none'.

classification.title: PARTIES TO ACTIONS GENERALLY
classification.summary: This statute outlines rules for parties involved in legal actions, specifically addressing situations with numerous parties and parties severally liable on contracts, bills, checks, and as sureties. It allows for one or more parties to sue or defend on behalf of a larger group if the question involves a common or general interest or if the parties are too numerous to be brought before the court practically. It also permits plaintiffs to include all or any parties severally liable on the same contract or instrument in a single action.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses general principles of civil procedure regarding parties to legal actions. It addresses how to handle cases with many plaintiffs or defendants and how to consolidate claims against parties with shared liability. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895 - Section 27 & 28
classification.summary: These sections of the 1895 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky deal with parties to actions. Section 27 outlines how actions can be brought against persons jointly bound by contract, allowing for actions against all or any of them, and specifying procedures when some parties are deceased. It also clarifies that a judgment against one or more jointly bound individuals does not bar proceedings against others. Section 28 addresses the court's power to require new parties to an action and provides examples of such situations, including cases involving property owners and cities, sub-lessees and assignees, and warranty claims. It also touches on bondholders' actions and the necessity of including indispensable parties like corporations.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text (Sections 27 and 28 of the 1895 Civil and Criminal Code) pertains to general legal procedures regarding parties in civil actions, specifically concerning joint contracts and the court's ability to add or require parties. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of statutes. Confidence is high because the text is purely procedural and lacks any racial component.

classification.title: Claimant of property may be made party - practice.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedure for a defendant in an action (upon contract or for recovery of personal property) to bring a third party into the lawsuit if that third party claims ownership of the subject of the action. The court can order the property to be secured, paid into court, or delivered to a designated person, and requires the claimant to appear and assert their claim. Failure to appear can result in the claimant being barred from future claims. The statute also includes case law citations related to this procedure.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895, specifically section 30, which deals with civil procedure regarding property claims. The statute describes a legal mechanism for resolving disputes when multiple parties claim ownership of the same property involved in a lawsuit. There is no mention of race, and the procedure described is a standard legal process applicable to all individuals regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Married women - actions by and against.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions under which married women can sue or be sued independently of their husbands. It specifies that a wife may sue or be sued alone in actions between husband and wife, concerning her separate property, or concerning her general property and personal suffering or injury to her person or character if her husband refuses to unite. In all other actions, the husband and wife must join. The statute also addresses situations where a husband deserts his wife, or when a female party to an action marries during its pendency.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the legal rights and procedures for married women in civil actions. It does not contain any language or provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it appears to be part of a compiled legal code from 1895. The confidence is high because the text clearly defines legal standing for married women without any racial component.

friend will be permitted to prosecute an
action without securing costs, as pro-
vided in sec.619. Westerfieldv .Wilson ,
12 Bush 125 ; and see sec .37-2 .
(6) Infant seeking to avoid contract
on ground of fraud can not avail himself
of his infancy unless he pleads it. Bryant
v .Pottinger, 6 Bush 473 .
(7) Next friendof infant female can
not prosecute an action in her name
against wishes of her infant husband .
Anderson v .Anderson , 11 Bush 327 ; and
where an infant plaintiff intermarries
with an infant husband it is proper to
enter him as coplaintiffand allow action
to proceed in name of her next friend as
next friend of both . Hopkins v .Virgin ,
11 Bush 677 .
(8 ) Next friendcan not commute debts
or judgments due infant. Forbes v .
Brents , 1 J. J. M. 441 ; and next friend
may be changed by leave of court. Burks
v .Shain ,2 Bibb 341.
(9) Non-residentguardianhas no power
to sue for the infant or otherwise act as
guardian in this State, unless authorized
by a county court of thisState,as pro-
vided by sec. 2041 Ky . Stat. (which is
copy of sec. 16, art. 2, chap . 48 , Gen.
Stat.). Watts v .Wilson , 93 Ky .495 ; and
see Williams v . Duncan , 92 Ky . 125 ;
Shelby v . Harrison ,84 Ky . 144.
(10 ) " Person of unsound mind ," as used
in this section, embraces not only luna-
tics but persons whose minds have be-
come so impaired by disease or other
cause as to be unable to take care of
their own interests. Howard v .Howard ,
87 Ky .616 .
(11) If a person alleged to be of un-
sound mind appears and proteststhat he
isnot incapable of managing his estate
and that suit is prosecuted without his
authority,the chancellor should impanel
a jury to pass upon the question of his
capacity. Howard v. Howard , 87 Ky .
616 .
(12) The trustee of a lunatic having
sold his land and appropriated the pro-
ceeds, a creditorof the lunatic sued his
sureties to recover the money . A suc-
cessorof the trustee,having subsequently
brought suit to have sale set aside as
fraudulent , it was held that he and not
creditorhad right to choose remedy and
maintain action . Trunk v . Eastern
Asylum , 6 R. 521.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895, Section 38
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines the procedures for bringing legal actions on behalf of individuals under disability, specifically adults who are imprisoned and have no resident curator, infants, and persons of unsound mind. It details who can initiate these actions, such as a curator, next friend, or guardian, and addresses various scenarios including non-resident guardians and the definition of a person of unsound mind. It also includes case law annotations related to these provisions.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text deals with legal procedures for individuals under disability (infants, persons of unsound mind, and imprisoned adults) and their representation in legal actions. It does not contain any language or provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code from 1895. The confidence is high because the content is clearly unrelated to racial segregation or discrimination.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895, Title III, Section 36
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for defendants under disability (infants, persons of unsound mind, adult prisoners) to have their defense made by a guardian, committee, curator, or guardian ad litem. It specifies that no judgment can be rendered against an infant (other than a married woman) or a person of unsound mind until their representative has made a defense or filed a report stating inability to do so. The accompanying notes discuss various court interpretations and applications of these provisions, particularly concerning infants.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses legal procedures for defendants under various disabilities, such as infancy or unsoundness of mind. It does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly targets or discriminates against any racial group. The provisions are procedural and apply to any individual falling under the defined categories of disability, regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Guardian ad litem - appointment— duties- compensation.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the appointment, duties, and compensation of a guardian ad litem for persons under disability in legal actions. It specifies the conditions for appointment, the qualifications of the guardian, and their responsibilities in preparing the case, including subpoenaing witnesses. It also details the liability of a guardian, curator, committee, or next friend for costs incurred during an action for a person under disability, unless allowed to sue in forma pauperis.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the appointment and duties of a guardian ad litem for individuals under legal disability. It is a procedural statute related to civil law and does not contain any provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895 - Service of Summons
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines the procedures for the return day of a summons, its delivery to an officer, and the daily attendance of the sheriff at the clerk's office. It also details who may serve a summons, the manner of service, and what facts must be stated in the return, along with penalties for non-compliance. Specific provisions are made for acknowledgment of service, service on counties, cities, corporations, carriers, infants under fourteen, persons of unsound mind, prisoners, Shakers, and non-residents.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details procedural aspects of serving legal summons in Kentucky courts in 1895. It covers the mechanics of summons delivery, return dates, and who is authorized to serve them. There is no mention of race, and the procedures described are general to all legal proceedings, not specific to any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

The statute is procedural in nature and applies to all individuals and entities within the jurisdiction of Kentucky courts. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895, § 58 [89] Affidavit for warning order - effect of.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the requirements for an affidavit to be filed when a warning order is sought in legal proceedings. It specifies who can make the affidavit (plaintiff, agent, attorney, guardian, etc.), the grounds for the warning order, and the information that must be included, such as the defendant's residence or place of business and nearest post-office, or a statement of ignorance of these facts. It also includes case law annotations related to the sufficiency and effect of such affidavits.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the procedural requirements for obtaining a 'warning order' in legal cases, which is a mechanism for notifying absent defendants. The text focuses entirely on the legal process, the types of affidavits required, and the information that must be present in them. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly discriminate based on race. The case law annotations also deal with procedural defects and sufficiency of affidavits, not racial matters. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895 - Service of Summons and Attorney Appointment
classification.summary: This section of the 1895 Civil and Criminal Code outlines procedures for serving summons, particularly when the plaintiff is absent or under disability. It details the requirements for affidavits supporting service and the appointment of attorneys for defendants who are constructively summoned. The clerk is to appoint a practicing attorney for the defendant, who must then make diligent efforts to inform the defendant of the action and report back to the court. Neither the plaintiff nor their attorneys can be appointed or suggest the defendant's attorney.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details procedural aspects of civil litigation, specifically concerning the service of summons and the appointment of attorneys for defendants. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that inherently suggest a racial discriminatory purpose or effect. The procedures described are standard legal mechanisms for ensuring due process in cases where a defendant cannot be personally served. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

It also touches upon procedural aspects like depositions and when an action stands for trial.

Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895, specifically § 62, outlines the rules for determining the county in which legal actions must or may be brought. It details provisions for actions concerning real property, including recovery, partition, sale, and injury to real property. It also includes case law interpretations regarding attachments, enforcement of contracts related to land, jurisdiction for selling infant's land, injuries to land, settlement of insolvent estates, land in adjoining counties, enforcement of liens, rescission of land contracts, sale of jointly held land, and specific execution of land contracts.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 and details the rules of venue for legal actions, particularly those involving real property. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute is purely procedural, defining where lawsuits can be filed based on the nature of the action and the location of property. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It also addresses scenarios involving bankrupt defendants and the implications for judgments against other defendants.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895 - Joinder of Actions
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines rules regarding the joinder of actions and the summoning of defendants. It specifies conditions under which a defendant cannot be summoned out of the county of their residence and rules for service of summons if a defendant moves after an action has commenced. It also details the criteria for joining multiple causes of action in a single lawsuit, such as requiring each cause to affect all parties, be brought in the same county, and be of the same kind of action.

Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is presented as part of a compiled code from 1895.

failure to make motion at proper time

Although misjoinderiswaived by notob-

ant summoned in another county , when

§ 87. (1) Allegationand proof. It is as
necessary to state a cause of action as itis
to sustain it by proof. The absence of
either prevents a recovery , Murrell v .
McAlister,79 Ky .311,and the plaintiff
can only recover upon proof of the cause
of action alleged in his petition. Gos-
som v .Badgett , 6 Bush 97 ; 1 Met . 339.
As to variance between pleading and
proof , see secs. 129, 130, 131, and notes
thereto .

admitted or a ground of defense not al-
leged in the pleadings,although proven ,is
to be treated as if such allegation or de-
fense did not exist . Wilkins v .Barnes ,
79 Ky .323 .
(3) Proof that a party introduces must
not contradict what he alleges in his
pleadings to be true, he will not be per-
mitted to recover on a state of facts he
says is not true ; he must amend his
pleadings. Payne v. Hardesty, 12 R.
336 .
(4) Constructionof pleadings. Before
verdict, pleadings will be construed
against the pleader ;ader ; afterverdict,plead-

count is just, due and unpaid is suffi-
cient. The debt could not be just if the
goods were not reasonably worth price

(3) Administrator- against. It isnot nec-

And before he can recover he must in-

(7) Award to setaside. To set aside

specificacts of fraud relied on must be
averred. Phillipsv. Phillips, 81 Ky .
147 .
(8) Blanks in petition. Plaintiff, in
order to recover specifiedamounts ,should
state the amounts , and not leave blank
places for them in his pleading . Jesse
v.Shuck , 11 R. 463.
(9) Bond. In actionon bond ,the peti-
tion should set out the covenants of the
bond ,and the breach of them relied on .
Mann v . Martin , 82 Ky . 242.
(10) In action on indemnifying bond ,
the terms and stipulations of the bond

(11) Petitionon injunctionbond con-
ditionedtopay judgment enjoined,ifin-
junction dissolved, is insufficientunless

Crawford v . Woodworth , 9 Bush 745.
(12) Constable- against . In action

fusalto pay. Harris v. Perry, 2 Bush
101 ; Huston v .Hagar , 1 Duv .24 .
(13) Contract- forbreach of. The breach
of a contract is an essentialpart of the
cause of action, and should be assigned
in the words of the contract , or in words
which are co -extensive with the import
and effect of it. The rules of pleading
which require that the contract shallbe
substantially set out , as well as the
breach of which plaintiffcomplains , are
fundamental , and have not been changed
by the Code . Moxley v . Moxley ,2 Met .
309 ; 8 B. M. 377. In an action for dam-
ages for breach of contract, where the
agreements are mutual and dependent ,
and each is to perform his part at the
same time , the petition must aver that
plaintiffwas ready to perform his part

ness to perform is only necessary where

N. R. R. v . Leavell, 16 B. M. 358. It is
necessary to state the contract, the
breach and the facts which show the loss
or damage by reason of the breach . Miles
v .Miller, 12 Bush 134 ; and see Lewis v .
Scott,95 Ky .484,for necessary allega-
tions in petitionby servant against mas-
ter to recover damages for wrongful dis-
charge .

judgment for his debt . Dobyns v .
Dobyns ,79 Ky . 95.

is not necessary to notice and remove

(19) Guaranty. When notice of the

quisitein actionon guaranty,the allega-

In action to surcharge settlement of

(21) Heirfordebtof ancestor. To make

As to proper averment that one is heir,

(23) Indorsersof note. In action upon
a note executed to plaintiffby D ,payable
in bank and indorsed in blank by F , N
and C before itsdelivery, whereby ,as al-
leged, they " intended to be equally
bound as obligors," it was not alleged

dation indorsers, or with the view of

Petition held not good against indorsers.

(24) The presumption isthat a note in
the hands of the maker of it, indorsed by
the payee , has been paid , and one to
whom the maker has discounted it can

averring that itwas indorsed for maker's
accommodation . Callahan v . First Nat .
Bank , 78 Ky .604 .
(25 ) Interest. To recover interest ac-
cruing anterior to the institutionof the
action on an unliquidated account , it is

(26) Interest is allowable as a matter

when ,by the contract, it is payable , or
from the time it becomes a liquidated
Henderson Mfg . Co. v . Lowell M.

one .

son.79 Ky .29.

nary and concise language the facts
constituting the cause of action, and
every fact necessary to enable the plaint-
iffto recover must be alleged,and every
essential averment required to make a
declaration good at common law must be
stated. The facts must be so alleged
as to enable the opposite party to know
what ismeant to be proved ,and alsothat
an issue may be formed in regard to the
subject matter in dispute and to enable
the court to pronounce the law upon the
facts as stated . L. & P. Canal Co. v .
Murphy ,9 Bush 522;Stiversv.Baker,87

(47) Prayer of petition. The petition
stated the amount of claims sued on , and
prayer was for judgment for the amount
of said claims, with interestdue on each .
Held sufficientto entitle plaintiff to
judgment forclaimsand interest. Har-
ris v . Perry , 2 Bush 101; and see fur
ther as to prayer for relief, 80 Ky .529;
80 Ky . 371 .
(48 ) The body of petition and prayer
must be consistent,and the prayer for
general relief must be consistent with
thatspecificallyprayed for. Crow v.O.
& N. R. R.,82 Ky .134.
(49) A party is not entitledto relief
that he does not ask for in his pleading .
Radford v . Southern Mutual Insurance
Co., 12 Bush 434 ; Howland v . Brown , 13
Bush 681 ; 11 Bush 49 .
(50) In an action to enforce a mortgage
lienthe prayer was ," Wherefore plaint
iffprays that the mortgage be foreclosed.
and for all other proper relief," no de
fense being made itwas error to render
a personaljudgment . Hansford v.Hol
dam , 14 Bush 210 .
(51) Prayer of petitionin actionto en
join collection of judgment " for all
proper relief" did not authorize judg-
ment against defendant for amounts
plaintiffclaimed to be entitledto. Walker
v . Thomas , 88 Ky .486 ; and see further
as to reliefthat may be granted under
prayer for " general relief," Lillard v .
Brannin,91 Ky .511; 80 Ky .529 ; 80 Ky ;
371. Alternative relief, see 9 R. 166 .
(52 ) Precedent condition. At common
law the pleader was required not only to
aver the performance of the condition
precedent , but to state the time and
manner of its performance . This rule
was changed by sec. 149 of Code of 1854.
Sec . 149 is omitted from this Code , and
its omission restores the common law
rule . Averbeck v . Hall , 14 Bush 505 ;

on their performance . Without such

(53) Promissory note. A petitionfounded
on a note must so set out the promise ,
its terms and its breach as to enable the
court to render a judgment by default
without being compelled to resortto the
note on file; a promise or agreement to
pay must be averred or petition will not
be good . Huffaker v . Nat . Bank of
Monticello , 12 Bush 287 ; see Hibler v .
Shipp ,78 Ky .64. In Totten v .Cooke , 2
Met . 275 , it is held that the failureto
state date of note sued on and filed with
petitiondoes not make petitiondefective,
if it state amount of note,time it ispay-
able and aver that defendant is indebted
in amount of note naming it, and see
Burton v . White , 1 Bush 9. The acci-
dental omission of the words " the de-
fendants ," the subject of the verb
"
agreed ," is not a substantial defect.
" The omission of those things which
are silently expressed is of no conse-
quence ." Warfield v . Gardner , 79 Ky .
583; and see Hibler v.Shipp,78 Ky .64.
(54 ) The petition must state when
note falls due , or so specify dates that
the fact may be ascertained without
going outside the pleading . Corbin v .
Oldham , 1 R. 327 .
(55 ) Slander. In action for slander, it
is necessary to set forth the specific
words complained of ; it is not sufficient
tostatethe effectof the words. Taylor
v . Moran , 4 Met . 126. And it is neces-
sary to aver that the words were spoken
maliciously. Williams v. Gordon, 11
Bush 693 .
(56) Splittingcause of action not allow-
able, and where one has sued for part of
an entire demand he will not be allowed
to sue for remainder in another action .
Pilcher v . Ligon ,91 Ky .228 ; Weinstock
v . Bellwood , 12 Bush 139 ; Powell v .
Weiler , 11 B. М. 187.
(57) Surety against co-surety. Action
can not be maintained by one surety
against a co-surety unless the principal
is insolvent , and this fact must be averred .
Bolling v .Doneghy , 1 Duv . 220 ; Lee v.
Forman , 3 Met .113.
(58) Surety againstprincipal. In action
by surety against principal to recover
money paid for him the petition alleged
that the note was paid in fullby plaintiff
as surety,and that no part of ithad been
repaid by defendant . The petition set
out a good cause of action on the implied
promise . Bridges v . Reed , 9 Bush 329.
(59) Tavern keeper- to enforce lien on
baggage. The petition must allege that
plaintiffis a tavern keeper . Allegation
that he " is a landlord,proprietor of the
Myers House ," is insufficient. South-
wood v . Myers , 3 Bush 681.
(60) Tax title. Petitionin action to
recover land under must allege that all
the statutory steps necessary to a valid
sale were taken . Jones v . Miracle , 14
R. 639 ; Durrett v . Stewart , 88 Ky . 665 ;
Com .v.Three Forks Coal Co. ,95 Ky .273.
(61) Title- to quiet. See Campbell v.
Disney , 93 Ky .41.

trator against husband of distributee,
the answer stated that no distribution
had been made and asked to be allowed
to retain amount as advancement ; held
not good because itdid not aver that ad-
ministrator had in his hands for distri-
bution an amount sufficient to entitle
each distributee to a sum equal to
amount of note. Haggard v.Hay ,13 В.
Μ . 174 .
(3) Amended petition. No answer is re-
quired to amended petition when the
denials of the answer to petitionare suf-
ficient,and apply as well to the amended
as to the originalpetition. Robinson v .
Williamson ,7 Bush 604 .
(4 ) Attachment withoutcause. In action
for suing out an attachment without
good cause the answer was " that the
grounds allegedby the defendant as con-
stituting the cause for its issual did in
fact,as he believes,existand were true ;"
held insufficient. Mitchell v . Mattingly ,
1 Met . 237 .
(5) Conclusionsof law. In action to re-

that defendant is an " innocent pur-
chaser " is merely a conclusion of law .
Wing v.Hayden ,10 Bush 276.
(6) In action against carrier for lossof
goods , averment in answer that accident
was unforeseen , inevitable or unavoid-
able, or that itwas caused by dangers of
the river, are conclusions of law and not
good ; the facts should be stated. Bent-
ley v.Bustard ,16 B. M. 643.
(7) In actionagainstsheriffforfailing

allthe property owned by execution de-
fendant was exempt from execution ,

Lay , 12 Bush 283.
(8) Conjunctivedenial. Denial in the con-
junctive is not good . Taylor v .Farmer ,
81 Ky .458 ; nor can two or more factsbe
grouped together and denied ; each ma-
terial fact must be separately denied .
Preston v . Roberts ,12 Bush 570 .
(9) Decedent'sestate. In suitforsettle-
ment of an estate, creditor may filepeti-
tion setting up his claim , and if claim
is controverted, has a right to demand
that it shall be done by answer , as in
other cases. Horner v .Harris , 10 Bush
357 .
(10) Defectiveallegationsinpetitioncured
by answer or verdict. It is a settled rule
of practice that defective allegationsof
the plaintiff'spleadingmay be supplied
and cured by the answer ;and the doctrine
of the Code is that verdicts and judg-
ments which have been rendered upon
substantial issuesof fact fairlypresented
by the pleadings shallnot be disturbed on
account of mere technical defects not
affecting the merits of the controversy .
Code , sec.134. Riggs v . Maltby , 2 Met
88 ; the failure to allege a fact that
would have rendered the petition bad
on demurrer will be cured by an answer
denying the existence of the fact. L. &
N. R. R. v. Lawson , 88 Ky .496 ; Pugh
v.White , 78 Ky . 210 ; 78 Ky . 435 ; 78
Ky .463; 13 Bush 601; 82 Ky . 291 ; but
if the petitionfailto allegematter which
is essential to make out a cause of action
the defect will not be cured by answer .
Bogenschutz v . Smith , 84 Ky . 330 ;
Drake v . Semonin , 82 Ky . 291.As to defects cured by verdict " The
rule is that when there is any defect, im-
perfection or omission ,even of substance ,
in a pleading which would have been
fatalon demurrer , yet if the issuejoined
be such as necessarily required on the
trial proof of the facts so imperfectly
stated or omitted , and without which it
isnot to be presumed the judge would
direct the jury to give, or that the jury
would have given ,the verdict,such defect
or omission is cured by the verdict."
Drake v . Semonin ,82 Ky . 291 ; L. & P.
Canal Co. v. Murphy , 9 Bush 522 ; the
rule is that all ambiguities and uncer

classification.summary: This section of the 1895 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines rules and case law regarding various types of legal responses (answers, counter-claims, set-offs, and cross-petitions) in civil litigation. It details requirements for these pleadings, such as averring facts, specifying defects, and the validity of different defenses like fraud, infancy, limitation, and lack of consideration. It also touches upon procedural aspects like inconsistent defenses and the plea of non est factum.

It details how defendants should respond to lawsuits, the types of defenses they can raise (e.g., fraud, infancy, statute of limitations), and the requirements for these defenses to be legally valid. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest a discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

arate action his claim . Sec . 17 ; Em-
merson v .Herriford ,8 Bush 229 ; 1 Duv .

lessamount than the demands of plaint-
iff, they should give credit for this

mainder. But if thedefendantsshould,
intheopinionof the jury,be entitledto
nothing,then the plaintiffshould have

dent's estate pleaded as a counter -claim

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895 - Title VII: Answer, Counter-Claim, Set-Off, Cross-Petition
classification.summary: This section of the 1895 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the legal procedures and definitions related to counter-claims, set-offs, and cross-petitions in civil litigation. It details what constitutes a set-off, the requisites for a counter-claim, and how these claims are handled during trials, including provisions for actions involving the Commonwealth and claims against decedents' estates. It also addresses set-offs against assignees and the assignment of notes for set-off purposes.

After a thorough review of the text, there are no mentions of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or application based on race. The content is purely procedural and relates to how defendants can assert claims against plaintiffs within a lawsuit. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This section of the 1895 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the legal procedures and conditions for filing a cross-petition and various types of set-offs in legal actions. It details when a defendant can bring a cross-petition against co-defendants or third parties, and when a defendant can use a set-off to reduce a claim against them, including equitable set-offs, set-offs in actions ex delicto, and set-offs related to contracts and debts. Several case examples illustrate specific scenarios and rulings on set-offs.

The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of statutes. The confidence is high because the content is purely procedural and lacks any racial dimension.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines rules for pleadings in legal actions, including counter-claims, set-offs, cross-petitions, rejoinders, and surrejoinders. It also details the timing for filing defenses and answers in ordinary and equitable actions, as well as provisions for extending time and filing in vacation. Section 101 specifically forbids departure from a stated cause of action or ground of defense in subsequent pleadings, with exceptions for equitable matters of avoidance or estoppel.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details procedural rules for legal pleadings and the timing of filing them within the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

It specifies what types of pleadings can be made as cross-petitions, set-offs, and counter-claims, and when replies and additional pleadings are allowed. It also provides general provisions concerning pleadings, including the ability to demur to part of a pleading and present an issue of fact as to another part, and that a pleading may contain multiple causes of action or defenses, which must be distinctly stated in separate, numbered paragraphs.

classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines rules for the verification of pleadings in legal proceedings. It details conditions under which an agent or attorney can verify a pleading if the party is absent, mentally or physically unable, or under a disability (except coverture or a combination of infancy and coverture). It also describes a process for a party to request that an adverse party verify their own pleading if there's a belief that a statement in the affidavit is untrue. Finally, it clarifies that verification does not increase the burden of proof for the adverse party.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details procedural rules for verifying legal pleadings in Kentucky civil and criminal cases. It addresses situations involving absent parties, those with disabilities, and joint pleadings. There is no mention of race, and the rules appear to apply generally to all litigants. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of statutes. The confidence is high because the text clearly pertains to legal procedure and lacks any racial component.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895 - General Rules of Pleading
classification.summary: This section of the 1895 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines general rules of pleading. It details what facts need not be stated in a pleading, such as evidence relied upon, presumptions of law, and facts of judicial notice. It also specifies how to plead a private statute by stating its title and the date it became law. Additionally, it mandates that writings on which an action is founded must be filed with the pleading.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the 'Civil and Criminal Code 1895' and details general rules of pleading. It discusses the requirements for stating facts in legal pleadings, how to refer to private statutes, and the necessity of filing documents that form the basis of an action. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any other element typically associated with Jim Crow laws. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines general rules of pleading, including requirements for filing documents with pleadings, striking out irrelevant or redundant matter, and how to plead judgments or determinations of courts or officers. It also includes case law annotations related to these rules.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 and details general rules of legal pleading. It discusses the filing of documents, striking out irrelevant text, and how to plead court judgments. There is no mention of race, and the rules appear to be procedural and applicable to all litigants. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895 - General Rules of Pleading
classification.summary: This section of the 1895 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines general rules of pleading for libel and slander actions. It specifies that extrinsic facts to show the application of defamatory matter to the plaintiff are unnecessary. Defendants can plead the truth of the alleged libel or slander and any mitigating circumstances to reduce damages. It also touches on pleadings for land recovery and judgments.

There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of statutes.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895 - General Rules of Pleading
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines general rules of pleading in civil actions, specifically concerning the recovery of land. It details what a defendant's answer must state regarding their claim to the land and addresses the admission of allegations not specifically traversed, with exceptions for parties under disability (except coverture). It also includes case law annotations related to these pleading rules.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text pertains to general rules of civil pleading, specifically in actions for the recovery of land. It details requirements for defendants' answers and the treatment of undisputed allegations. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is presented as part of a compiled code from 1895.

classification.title: INTERROGATORIES
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines rules and procedures for using interrogatories in legal actions. It details when amendments to pleadings can be made without verification, how answers to interrogatories in equitable and ordinary actions are treated (often as depositions), the timeframes for answering, and the consequences of failing to answer or verify. It also covers compelling parties to appear as witnesses and how claims or defenses can be sustained by a failure to answer.

There is no mention of race, and the procedures described are general legal mechanisms applicable to all parties in a lawsuit. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code.

It details the necessary components of an affidavit for arrest, including the belief that the plaintiff ought to recover a certain sum, and specific grounds for arrest such as the defendant intending to defraud creditors by departing the state and concealing or moving property, or departing without leaving sufficient property to satisfy the claim. It also specifies the bond requirements for the plaintiff to cover potential damages if the order is wrongfully obtained, the requisites of the order of arrest itself, and its return day.

classification.title: Liability and Discharge of Bail and of Officer

It details how bail can surrender a defendant, how their liability is fixed by a "not found" return on an execution, and the conditions for exoneration such as the defendant's death, removal from the state as a fugitive, imprisonment in the penitentiary, or legal discharge from obligations. It also specifies that actions against bail must be separate.

It also covers motions to vacate or reduce an order of arrest.

classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for obtaining an order for the delivery of personal property to a plaintiff in a legal action. It requires an affidavit from the plaintiff detailing the property, its value, damages for detention, and the plaintiff's ownership or special interest and right to immediate possession. The accompanying case law provides interpretations on when an action can be maintained, the necessity of demand, consequences of property destruction, vendor's rights in fraudulent purchases, identity of property, joinder of joint owners, and the form of judgments in such cases.

Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

that they have no propertyinthisState
subjecttoexecutionand the collectionof

value of defendant's property , the court

classification.title: Grounds of Attachment
classification.summary: This section outlines the legal grounds for issuing an attachment against a debtor's property. These grounds include absence from the state for a specified period, departure with intent to defraud creditors, avoidance of summons service, concealment, removal or disposition of property to hinder creditors, and insolvency. It also details evidentiary requirements and legal precedents for each ground.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the legal grounds for issuing an attachment in civil cases. It outlines conditions related to a debtor's absence, intent to defraud, concealment, and financial status. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it appears to be part of a compiled legal code from 1895.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895 - Attachment Procedures
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 details the requirements and procedures for obtaining and executing attachments, including the necessary bond from the plaintiff to cover potential damages to the defendant if the attachment is wrongfully obtained. It outlines the conditions under which damages can be recovered on the bond, such as loss of property use, deterioration, and defense costs, and distinguishes these from damages incurred in the main action. Attorney's fees are recoverable under specific conditions. The statute also specifies that an action on the bond can only be maintained after the attachment has been discharged, and it differentiates between actions on the bond and actions for maliciously suing out an attachment, the latter requiring proof of want of probable cause and malice.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses legal procedures related to attachments and bonds in civil and criminal cases. It outlines the financial responsibilities and legal recourse available to parties involved in attachment proceedings. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is presented as part of a compiled code from 1895. The confidence is high because the text is purely procedural and lacks any racial dimension.

§ 203. (1) Acknowledgment of attachment

§ 221.(1) Bond to dischargeattachment-
effect of. When this bond is executed , all
power of the court and its officers over
the attached property ceases, and the
plaintiffcan look only to the bond . Bell
v . Western River Co. , 3 Met . 557. The
attachment is discharged by operation
of law , and the obligorsin the bond are
bound unconditionally to perform the
judgment of the court in the action. In
an action to enforce the bond , neither the
sufficiencyof the grounds of the attach-
ment nor the liabilityof the property
levied on can be inquired into, nor can
the claims which any person may have
to the attached property be investigated.
Hazelrigg v.Donaldson ,2 Met . 445 ; Tay-
lor v . Taylor , 3 Bush 118 ; Inman v .
Stratton, 4 Bush 445 ; Bell v . Western
River Co. ,3 Met . 557.
(2) The property attached was owned
by the surety in the bond executed under
this section, but the surety could not
resist the collection of the bond on that
ground , nor on the ground that he had
offered the sheriff a forthcoming bond
as provided in sec. 214, which he had a
right to execute , but the sheriffrefused
to accept . Hazelrigg v . Donaldson , 2
Met . 445 .
(3) Collectionof by rule. Collectionof
bonds taken under this section may be
enforced by rule. Leet v . Lockett , 4
Met . 56 ; 12 Bush 416 ; sec. 232. But
notice should be given to the obligors in
the bond before judgment isgiven against
them . Taylor v .Taylor ,3 Bush 118 .
(4 ) Forthcoming bond may be executed
by defendant or person in possessionof
property , as provided in sec.214, and any
defenses that may exist to grounds of
attachment or to the ownership of the
property levied on can be made on trial
of attachment but not in action on bonds .
Schwein v . Sims , 2 Met . 209 ; Miller v .
Desha , 3 Bush 212; Hobson v. Hall, 13
R. 109 .
(5 ) Must be executed incourt, if court in
session. Bond discharging the attach-
ment must be executed in court , if the
court is in session ; and such a bond ,
taken by the sheriff when the court is
in session , can not be treated as a statu-
tory bond . The Louisville Chancery
Court is always in session, and a bond
taken by the sheriffof Jefferson county ,
to discharge an attachment issued by
the LouisvilleChancery Court,can not
be collectedby rule. LouisvilleR. R.
Co. v . Masonic Bank ,12 Bush 416 .
(6) Replevyingjudgmentreleasessurety.
A surety in a bond given under this sec-
tionisreleasedfrom liabilityby theex-
ecutionof a bond replevyingthe judg-
ment rendered against the defendant .
Gray v . Merrill, 11 Bush 633 .

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895, Section 244-248
classification.summary: These sections of the 1895 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outline the forms of judgments in actions brought by a surety against their principal, or by a joint debtor against a co-debtor. They detail how money of the defendant under the court's control should be applied to satisfy judgments, including those rendered before the demand has matured, with provisions for interest abatement. Section 247 also specifies that judgments include costs and can be enforced as in equitable actions, with specific endorsements for executions in favor of the plaintiff for the benefit of the creditor. Section 248 requires the plaintiff to pay the remaining portion of the creditor's demand or provide proof of payment/release before a judgment in their favor is rendered.

It outlines how attachments can be controverted, the trial process for attachments, the grounds for discharging an attachment, and the appeal process for orders related to attachments. The text focuses on legal procedures and evidence related to property attachments in civil litigation.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text pertains to civil procedure regarding attachments, which are legal mechanisms to seize property to secure a debt or judgment. The text discusses legal processes, evidence, and court actions. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1895.

classification.title: INJUNCTION - WHEN AND HOW OBTAINED
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines the conditions under which a temporary injunction may be granted. It covers situations such as preventing acts that would render a judgment ineffectual, illegal taxes or assessments, nuisances, prosecutions under invalid ordinances, restraints of trade, protection of property title, and trespass. The text also references numerous case law examples to illustrate these points.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the legal procedures and circumstances for obtaining an injunction in Kentucky civil and criminal cases. It focuses on the equitable remedy of injunctions to prevent harm or ensure the effectiveness of legal judgments. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of statutes.

entries.ocr_text:
TITLE VIII] INJUNCTION—WHEN AND HOW OBTAINED. 159

§ 273. **Officers who may grant.** The injunction may be granted at the commencement of the action, or at any time before judgment by the court, or by any circuit judge, or by the clerk of the court, or the county judge if the judge of the court be absent from the county, or by two justices of the peace if the judge and the clerk of the court and the county judge be absent from the county. No injunction shall be granted by any of the foregoing officers, unless it appear from the affidavit of the party applying therefor that it has not been refused by the court; nor shall such injunction be granted by a clerk or county judge or justice unless it appear from such affidavit that it has not been refused by the court or any circuit judge. No injunction or temporary restraining order shall be granted until after the petition seeking the injunction shall be filed. No injunction or temporary restraining order shall be granted by any circuit or other judge of similar jurisdiction in any action pending outside of the circuit or county wherein such judge shall preside, unless it shall appear from the affidavit of the plaintiff that there is no circuit or other judge of similar jurisdiction present at the time in the county where the action is pending. (*Sec. as amended by act 1894.*)

§ 274 [302] **Granted before judgment—court may control.** Injunctions granted before judgment shall be subject to the further order of the court.

§ 275 [304] **Defendant who has answered to be notified.** An injunction shall not be granted against a defendant who has answered, unless he have had notice of the application therefor.

§ 276 [305] **Notice of application for—when not required.** An injunction shall be granted only upon reasonable notice, in writing, to the party sought to be enjoined, of the time and place of the application therefor, and of the court or officer to whom the application is to be made. Where the court or officer to whom the application for an injunction is made shall be satisfied by the facts set forth in the affidavit of the applicant, or by other evidence, that irreparable injury will result to the applicant from the delay of giving notice, the court or officer may enter a temporary order restraining the act or acts sought to be enjoined, or it may be mandatory in its nature, if the case so require, except that no mandatory restraining order shall be entered by any clerk, county judge or two justices in any case. Such restraining order shall in no case be granted until the applicant shall have given bond, as prescribed by subsection three of section

(12) Waste may be restrained. Ky. Stat., sec. 2336.
274. Order refusing temporary injunction is not final. Dulaney v. Murphy, 12 R. 688.

classification.title: Dissolution and Reinstatement of Injunction
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures and limitations for dissolving or modifying injunctions in Kentucky civil cases. It specifies that only one motion to dissolve or modify an injunction is allowed per case. Upon dissolution, damages may be assessed by the court, either summarily or by a jury, particularly when an injunction stayed proceedings on a judgment. If money collection or property delivery was enjoined, damages can include a percentage of the sum released or the value of the use/hire/rent of the property, not exceeding ten percent. The statute also details the time allowed for seeking reinstatement of a dissolved or modified injunction.

Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it appears to be part of a compiled legal code from 1895.

§ 298[328]Courtmay appoint- appealfromorder. On the motion of any
party to an actionwho shows thathe has,or probablyhas,a rightto,
a lienupon, or an interestin, any propertyor fund,the rightto
which isinvolvedintheaction,and thatthe propertyor fund isin
danger of beinglost, removed or materiallyinjured,the court, or
thejudgethereofduringvacation,may appointa receiverto take
chargeof thepropertyorfund duringthe pendency of the action,
and may orderand coercethe deliveryofitto him . The order of a
court,or of thejudge thereof,appointingor refusingto appointa
receiver,shallbe deemed a finalorderforthepurposeofan appealto
the CourtofAppeals:Provided, That suchordershallnotbe super-
seded .
§ 298. (1) Actionagainstreceivercan not
be brought without leave of court by
which he was appointed . Spalding v .
Com .,88 Ky . 135.
(2) Appointment of powers. In an ac-
tion under the act of 1856 , the statute
does not authorize the appointment of
a receiver, except to take charge of
property in the possession, or under the
control,of the transferee, or the person
to whom the conveyance is made ; and
there is no law authorizing a receiver to
take charge of property in the possession
of the transferrer, until ithas been first
adjudged that the conveyance operated
as an assignment . Griffith v . Cox , 79
Ky .562 .
(3) When a receiverhas been appointed
and is in possession, his possession is the
possession of the court, and may not be
disturbed without leave of the court ;
nor will the court, without its sanction
be firstobtained ,permit any one to inter-
cept or prevent payment to its receiver
of anything which he has been ap-
pointed to receive, although itmay not
be actually in his hands . Hazelrigg v.
Bronaugh , 78 Ky .62 .
(4) Under section299 a receivermay
be appointed to preserve the property ,
but not to sellit. Wilson v .A. & T. Co. ,
91 Ky .299.
(5) See further, Kentucky Statutes,
sec .392 , as to receivers .
(6) Liabilityfor funds. The receiver of
a fund having loaned it to a firm of
which he was a member ,the other mem-
bers of the firm are liable, although the
money was repaid to the receiver by the
firm. Ryan v . Morrill,83 Ky . 352.
(7) Not allowedtoderiveprofitfrom funds.
A receiver will not be permitted to speс-
ulate in, or derive any profit or benefit
from , the purchase of property under his
control or in his hands ; and any pur-
chase or profitso made will inure to the
benefitof the partiesin interest. Tither-
ington v .Hodge ,81 Ky . 286 .
(8) Order refusingto appoint receiveris
final and may be appealed from . Lea-
vell v .Poore , 91 Ky . 321.

classification.summary: This section of the 1895 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the procedures for trial by jury, including the formation of the jury, the order of proceedings, and the delivery and definition of verdicts. It references existing statutes for jury summoning, impaneling, challenging, and swearing, and details the court's discretion in trial order and argument conclusion. It also touches upon continuances and the burden of proof.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details the procedural aspects of jury trials in Kentucky's Civil and Criminal Code of 1895. It focuses on the mechanics of the legal process, such as jury selection, trial order, and verdict delivery. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

plaintiff'stestimony , and afterward be-
comes satisfiedthat thiswas error,itmay ,
upon conclusion of allthe evidence , set

(14) The rule is, " that a party may
move upon the evidence of his antagon-
ist, assuming that evidence and every
fact which it conduces to prove as true,
and ask the court for a peremptory in-
struction; but he can not make such a

§ 318. View of premises. Court may
permit jury to view place where an acci-
dent occurred , even after they have re-
tired to consider their verdict, when
the jury so request. L., N. A. & C. R.
R. v .Schick , 94 Ky . 191 ; and see Kу . С.

particular time during trial.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895, Section 318
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines the procedure for a jury to view property or a place relevant to a case. It specifies that the court may permit such a view if it deems it proper, and provides guidance on when this can occur, even after the jury has retired to deliberate, if requested by the jury. It also references case law related to this procedure.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895 - Trial by Jury
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines the procedures for the discharge of a jury, retrial of cases where a jury is discharged, and the manner of rendering and finalizing a verdict. It details when a jury can be discharged (sickness, accident, consent, or inability to agree), the process of polling the jury to confirm their verdict, and the requirements for a verdict to be considered complete. It also references a provision for when three-fourths of the jury can render a verdict.

" The meaning of the expression 'sep

It also includes case law citations related to verdict interpretation and curing defects.

Amendment of May 15, 1866. So much of chapter two , title
nine,of the CivilCode of Practiceas authorizesand providesfor
a separate-generalor a specialverdictbe,and thesame ishereby,
repealed: Provided, That the court in itsdiscretionmay direct
the jury to finda separate-generalverdict.
at the same time the evidence upon the
issue of ratificationmight be such that
the preponderance in favor of a ratifica-
tion would be so overwhelming that the
court should grant a new trial. If a
separate-general verdict is asked for,the
court shall grant it, and with itshallre-
quire the jury to return a general ver-
dict . In such cases it isnot only proper ,
but necessary, that the jury should be
instructed by the court as to the law
applicable to the particular issuesto be
found by the jury as fully as if there
was only one issue in the case and a jury
were required to find a general verdict
thereon." Witty v.C. & O. R. R. Co.,
83 Ky . 21 .
SPECIAL VERDICTS .
(1) Specialfindingspermissible. In Tins-
ley v .Ross ,15 R. 44,special findingswere
submitted to the jury , and on their find-
ings the court rendered judgment . In
answer to the objection that the law per-
mitting special findings had been re-
pealed,and it was therefore error to sub-
mit them , the court held the objection
not available as the judgment was cor-
rect .
Specialfindings- practice. When in an
ordinary action both legal and equitable
defenses are reliedupon , the court may
submit to the jury for itsdecision such
matters of fact as are involved in the
equitable issue, when such issue has not
been transferred to the equity docket ,
and the finding of the jury in relationto
the facts involved in the equitable issue
should be special, to enable the court to
render such a judgment thereon as may
be proper and equitable between the par-
ties. Petty v . Malier, 15 В. М. 591 .
(2) The finding of a jury on a special
verdict as to the evidence will be treated
like a general verdict, and will not be
disturbedunlessflagrantlyagainst the
evidence . Empire Co. v . McIntosh , 82
Ky . 554 .
(3) When there is no evidence to sup-
port a special verdict,the court, instead
of rendering judgment against, it should
grant a new trial. Casey v . L. & N. R.
R. Co.,84 Ky . 79 ; L. & N. R. R. Co. v .
Brice ,84 Ky . 298.
" The rule isimperative that the court
shall direct the jury to find upon such
issues or facts as are embraced by the
motion or interrogatories, but the court
may rejectthe form proposed by counsel
and put the question or prescribe the
mode of eliciting the special verdict
according to its sound discretion ." Louis-
villeR. R. v . Weams ,80 Ky .420 .
(4) In directing a special verdict the
court should confine the questions pro-
pounded to the controlling facts in the
case and they should be such as to enable
the court on the return of the verdict to
apply the law and enter judgment with-
out anything further from the jury ;
and where either party may be entitled
to recover money , or where damages are
to be assessed, the court should direct
the jury to assessthe amount of recovery.
A special verdict is a finding of facts
entries.citation: Civil and Criminal Codes of Practice of Kentucky, 1895, p. 187

§ 329[359]Assessmentofrecoverybyjury. If,by a generalverdict,either

theirverdict,must assessthe amount of recovery.
§ 330[360]When juryto assessvalueof propertyand damages. In actions
forthe recoveryof specificpersonalproperty,thejury must assess

issue. Witty v . C. & O. R. R. Co., 83
Ky . 21 ; Berry v . Pusey , 80 Ky . 166 ; Im-
perial Ins.Co. v . Kiernan , 83 Ky . 468.
(5) Special verdict to control. Where
special findingsare directed,it should be
to every fact necessary to make out a
cause of action or defense ; if they are
complete they must control, and the
court will not look to the general verdict
or the instructionsthat may have been
given to the jury . The questions of law
may be reserved by the court until after
verdict. L. & N. R. R. Co. v . Brice,84
Ky .298 ;Adams v . L. & N. R. R. Co.,82
Ky . 603.
§ 328. Generaland specialverdict. If a
general and a special verdict are incon-
sistent,judgment shall be rendered pur-
suant to the latter, but the judgment
should be rendered on the general verdict
when the special findings are not incon-
sistentwith the general verdict. A bill
of evidence is not necessary to enable the
court to determine whether or not a gen-
eraland a special verdict are inconsist-
ent. Quaid v .Cornwall ,13 Bush 601 ; see
Gailbraith v . Arlington Ins. Co., 12
Bush 29 .
§ 329. Jury to assess amount of recovery
-exceptions to rule. Questions of value
and damages are questions of fact, and
as a rule in cases involving such ques-
tions the jury must assessthe amount of
recovery ; but in an action on a note
where the only defense was usury and
verdict was , " we of the jury find for the
plaintiff," a judgment for the amount of
the note and interestwas proper . Cooper
v .Poston , 1 Duv . 92 ; and see Brannin v .
Foree , 12 B. M. 506, and see notes 1-5 to
sec . 126 .
§ 330. (1) Verdict and judgment in ac-
tionforrecoveryof property. In an action
for the recovery of specific personal
property , the jury should fix in their
verdict the value of the property and the
damages for itsdetention ; and the judg-
ment should be in the alternative for the
return of the property or itsvalue in case
a return can not be had , and damages
for the taking and withholding of the
property . Rogers v . Bradford , 8 Bush
163 ; Young v .Parsons ,2 Met . 499 ; Bates
v . Buchanan , 2 Bush 117 ; Strubbee v .
C. R. W. Co. , 78 Ky .481 ; and see notes
to sec . 181 .
(2) To authorize a jury to assessdam-
ages for property illegallytaken and de-
tained , there should be some evidence of
the value , or some description of the
property ,to enable the jury to determine
the value from their own knowledge .
Pharis v . Carver , 13 B. М. 237.

§ 331. (1) Court,trialby- motionfornew
trial. In a common law action , where the
law and facts are submitted to the court,
a motion and grounds for a new trialare
necessary in order to bring before the
Appellate Court any errorscommitted by
the court during the progress of the trial.
Helm v .Coffey,80 Ky .176 ; Henderson v .
Dupree ,82 Ky .678 ; Harper v . Harper ,
10 Bush 447; see further, 95 Ky . 60 .
(2)Judgment - how regarded. When the
law and facts are submitted to the court ,
every fact which goes to support the
judgment must be assumed tohave been
found in favor of the party for whom
judgment was given ,and every fact as to
which the evidence is conflicting must
be assumed to have been found against
the adverse party . Coleman v . Meade ,
13 Bush 358 ; and see note 1, sec. 10.
(3) The submissionof thelaw and facts
to the court clothes the court with all the
rightsand functions of a jury in determin-
ing the facts, and in the assessment of
damages ; and the judgment will not be
disturbed upon the ground that it is
against the evidence for any lessreason
than would authorize the setting aside of
the verdict of a jury . Baum v . Win-
ston , 3 Met . 127 ; Mulholland v . Sam-
uels , 8 Bush 63 .
(4 ) See notes 1-5, inclusive, to sec. 126
as to assessment of value and damages
by the court.
(5) Waiver of jury trial. Waiver of a
trialby jury should appear in the entry
submitting the facts to the court ; or it
may be inferred from an entry showing
that the court heard testimony and pro-
nounced judgment thereon ; but such in-
ference can not be indulged in where
nothing appears but a judgment by de-
fault. Clarke v .Seaton , 18 B. M. 226. It
was held that where a party appeared
and filedan insufficientanswer , the fail-
ure to plead further was not a waiver of
his right to a trialby jury . Burgess v .
Jacobs , 14 B. M. 517. But the transfer
of a caseby consent to the equity docket
is a waiver of a trialby jury . Smith v .
Moberly ,15 В. М. 70 .
(6)Weightgiventojudgment. Where the
law and facts are submitted to the court ,
itsfindingsof factsare to be treated as the
verdict of a properly instructed jury ,
and will not be disturbed unlesspalpably
against the evidence . Bell v . Wood , 87
Ky .56 ; and see note 1 to sec. 10 .

§ 333 [363] Exception- how taken- objectionwhen necessary. 1.An excep-
tionisan objectiontakento a decisionof thecourtupon a matterof
law .
trialof provisionalremedies. Francisv.
Burnett,84 Ky .24; Haynes v. Wiley,12
R. 299. See further,95 Ky .60.
(2) When the court upon request states
separately its conclusions of law and
fact, there must be an exception to the
conclusions of law ; a motion for a new
trial, in the absence of such exception ,
will not cure omission . American Aid
Society v .Bronger , 91 Ky . 406 .
(3) When neither the law nor the facts,
nor both combined , authorize the judg-
ment ,no separation is necessary . Com .
v.King ,86 Ky .436 ; nor is it necessary
to separate conclusions of law and fact
inan equitycase. Hartford Ins.Co. v .
Haas , 87 Ky . 531 ; nor when an agreed
state of facts is submitted to the court .
City of Owensboro v . Weir ,95 Ky . 158.
§ 333. (1) Amendment. An exception
taken to the filingof an amendment that
isallowed to be filedwithout objection is
not available; objection should have
been made to the filing. Helburn v.
Mofford,7 Bush 169.
(2) Demurrer. An errorin overruling
a demurrer must be excepted to at the
time. Beaven v.Phillips,83 Ky .88.
(3) Depositions. When an exception
goes to the whole of a deposition, and
some of it is competent , it should be
overruled . L. & N. R. R. Co. v . Graves ,
78 Ky .74 ; see further, sec. 586.
(4 ) When depositions are excepted to,
it must appear that the exception was
acted on , or it will not avail. Corn v .
Sims , 3 Met . 391 ; Lewis v . Wright , 3
Bush 311 ; Walrath v .Viley , 1 Bush 266.
(5) Evidence. A party must object to
the introduction of incompetent evi-
dence , and , if it is allowed over his
objection, must except to it at the time ,
or the error is waived . Crabb v .Larkin ,
9 Bush 154; Helburn v .Mofford , 7 Bush
169 ; Russell v . Marks ,3 Met . 37 ; L. &
N. R. R. Co.v.Graves,78 Ky .74 ;where
an objection was entered to the admission
of evidence , and at the close of the evi-
dence the bill of exceptions stated " to
all of which the defendant excepted ,"
held sufficientexception . L. & N. R. R.
v .Ritter, 85 Ky . 368.
(6 ) A decision of the court in exclud-
ing testimony must be excepted to at
the time , or the error is waived . Terrill
v . Jennings , 1 Met .450 .

2.A partymay ,withouta previousobjection,exceptto a decis-
ion againsthim ,unlessit be made at the instanceof the adverse
party .
3. But a partycan not exceptto a decisionmade at the instance
of theadverseparty,unlessobjectionshallhavebeenmade to the

§ 334[364]Exceptionwhen tobetaken- timetopreparebill. The party ob-
jectingmust exceptwhen the decisionismade ; and time may be
given toprocurea billof exceptions,but not beyond a day in the
succeedingterm,to be fixedby the court. (Form of bill,page 645.)
(7 ) Finalorders need not be excepted to.
Final judgments ,whether upon issuesof
law or fact, need not be excepted to.
Where a demurrer to an answer is sus-
tained and judgment rendered against
the defendant , or where a peremptory
instruction is given , no exception is
necessary. Coffman v. Wilson , 2 Met .
542; Craycraft v . Duncan , 6 R. 651 ;
Loving v .Warren Co.,14 Bush 316. Nor
isit necessary to except to a void order .
Smith v . Blakeman , 8 Bush 476 .
(8) Instructions- exceptions to. Al-
though instructionsmay be objected to,
if no exception is taken to the action of
the court in giving them , their correct-
ness can not be questioned . L. & Ν .
R. R. Co. v . Graves , 78 Ky . 74 ; Cox v .
Winston , 3 Met . 577 ; Reed v . Com ., 7
Bush 641 ;Garrot v . Ratliff, 83 Ky . 384.
(9) The exception to an instruction
must be taken at the time it is given ,
and the circuitcourt can not adopt any
rule of practice in contravention of the
provisions of the Code requiring excер-
tions and objections to be taken and
made as therein directed. Kennedy v .
Cunningham , 2 Met . 538 ; Letton v .
Young , 2 Met . 558 .
(10 ) An objection must be made to the
giving of an instructionon the motion of
the adverse party ,as well as an exception
taken if it isgiven , or the error will not
be considered. Loving v . Warren Co.,
14 Bush 316 ; Long v . Hughes , 1 Duv .
387 ; Forest v .Crenshaw , 81 Ky .51 .
(11) Objectionand exception. Unless an
objection is firstmade , an exception to a
decision made at the instance of the ad-
verse party is unavailing . Loving v .
Warren Co. , 14 Bush 316.
(12) Peremptoryinstruction- neitheran
objection or exception is necessary to
enable a party to complain of. Loving
v .Warren Co. , 14 Bush 316 ; 2 Met .542 .
(13) Rule of court that exception need
not be taken at time is not valid . Ken-
nedy v . Cunningham , 2 Met .538 .
(14) Waiver of error. Neither party
can rely for reversal upon an erroneous
decision made at the instance of the ad-
verse party unless he objected to the mo-
tion or offerof the adverse party at the
time itwas made , and then excepted to
the decision ; but where the court acts
upon itsown motion an exception alone
is sufficient. Branson v . Com ., 92 Ky .
330 .
§ 334. (1) Billcan not be filedinvacation.
A bill of exceptions can not be filedin
vacation in the clerk'soffice, nor at any
time except during term time . Nor can
this rule be changed by an order of
court ; and itisquestionable ifby agree-
ment of parties, entered of record, a bill
filed in vacation can be considered .
Allard v .Smith , 2 Met .297 ; Freeman v .
Brenham , 17 B. M. 603 ; and see Van-
dever v . Griffith, 2 Met . 425 ; Corley v .
Evans , 4 Bush 409 .
(2) Deathof oneof the partiesbeforebill
filed. The death of the appellee before
the time allowed for filingthe billof ex-
ceptions will not prejudice the appellant
if his bill be tendered within the time
allowed ; although there may have been
no revivor. Hayden v.Ortkeiss,83 Ky .
396 .
