(3) JeffersonCourt of Common Pleas.
The Jefferson Court of Common Pleas
has the same power over its judgments
for sixty days that the circuitcourts
have during the term . If under any cir-
cumstances the court is authorized to ex-
tend the time to filea billto a day beyond
a period of sixtydays from the judg-
ment ,it can not be filedafter the day so
fixed if no notice is taken of the matter
on that day . Bailey v. Villier, 6 Bush
27. If time be extended by that court
beyond sixtydays from date of judg-
ment ,and the billis filedwithin the time
allowed without objection, the error, if
any ,will be regarded as waived . Down-
ing v.Bacon , 7 Bush 680 ; see further,
Johnson v.Stivers, 95 Ky . 128; holding
that not more than one hundred and
twenty days can be allowed after judg-
ment to file bill .
(4) Nunc pro tunc order that " the par-
tiescame by their attorneys, and it ap-
pearing at the trialof this cause at the
last term of this court the court allowed
defendants time to prepare a bill of ex-
ceptions," and thereupon " the billwas
filedas part of the record," was invalid,
as no time had been given at a former
term to file the bill. Vandever v . Grif-
fith,2 Met .425 .
(5) Pendency of motion for new trial-
effectof. The time for filinga billof ex-
ceptions is reckoned from the overruling
of the motion for a new trial, and not
from the rendition of the judgment on
the verdict, and the court has no power
pending the motion for a new trial to
prescribe the time within which the bill
of exceptions shall be filed; and where
a judgment was rendered at the March
term ,1873 ,and the motion for a new trial
not finallydisposed of until the March
term ,1874,a billof exceptions fileddur-
ing latter term was in time . Harper v .
Harper , 10 Bush 447.
(6) Record must show time was given. If
time is given in which to prepare and
filea billof exceptions, the record must
show that the time was given , or it can
not be filed,although by neglect or over-
sight the order giving time was not
made . Vandever v .Griffith,2 Met .425 ;
Linch v . Reynolds , 6 Bush 547.
(7) Special judge . When a special
judge ,who has trieda case,extends time
for filingbill to a day in next term , and
isnot present on that day ,the party who
then tenders bill is entitled to have offer
to filecontinued to succeeding term , and
then have bill signed by special judge .
McFarland v .Burton , 89 Ky . 294.
(8) Stenographer'sreportof testimony. It
was held in McAllister v . Conn . Life Ins .
Co.,78 Ky .531, that the stenographer's
report of evidence , unless identified in
the bill of exceptions,would not be re-
garded as part of the record, but see
now ,as to stenographers in certaincoun-
ties,Ky .Stat.,sec.4637 .
(9) Tenderof billto judgeis equivalent
to filing, and it may be tendered to him
before day fixed for filing, and be filed
by him subsequent to that day . Meaux
v .Meaux ,81 Ky .475.
(10) Time to filebillof exceptions. The
defendant was given until the third day
of the succeeding term to prepare and
filea billof exceptions. On that day no
notice was taken of the matter , and on
the fourth day an order was made
extending the time in which to filebill
to the eighth day . On motion the bill
was stricken from the record . The bill
should have been filed on or before the
third day , or further time to a day cer-
tain given on that day . L. & N. R. R.
Co. v .Turner ,81 Ky . 489. See Freeman
v .Brenham , 17 B. M. 603.
(11) Time was given untila day in the
next term to filebillof exceptions, and
on the day specified an order was made
givingfurther time. Two days afterward
the bill was filed. The bill was not con-

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895, Section 338-339

They outline rules for how exceptions should be made, signed by judges, and how their truth can be controverted by affidavits. It also includes case law annotations regarding the signing and impeachment of bills of exceptions.

dered . Dickinson v .Trout , 8 Bush 441 .

(4 ) When a party seeks tohave a judg-

to reconsider the facts or law out of
which they are alleged to have grown .
The law does not mean that the grounds
for new trialshall contain a particular
description of the errors relied on ,but
that the particular errorsshallbe simply
pointed out or indicated in a common-
sense way ." L. & N. R. R. Co. v .McCoy ,
81 Ky .403 ; Meaux v .Meaux ,81 Ky .475 .
(14) While the general statement that
verdict is against law and evidence isnot
sufficient,yet when the lower court has
granted a new trial upon such a state-
ment the court will not reverse the second
judgment and direct firstto be entered
for the reason that the grounds upon
which new trialwas granted were insuf-
ficient. L. & N. R. R. Co. v . Coniff, 16
R. 296 .
(15) Excessive damages - remitter of
judgment. It has been repeatedly held
by the court that a new trial would not
be granted because of excessive damages ,
unless the damages should be so great as
to strike the mind at firstblush as having
been superinduced by passion or preju-
dice. L. & N. R. R. v.Mitchell,87 Ky .
327 ; Standard Oil Co. v . Tierney , 92 Ky .
367 ; Letton v . Young , 2 Met . 558. and
see Kountz v . Brown , 16 В. М. 577 ; М.
& L. R. R. Co. v . Herrick , 13 Bush 122 ;
Shirley v .Billings, 8 Bush 147 : L. & Ν .
R. R. Co. v . Fox , 11 Bush 495 ; L. & N.
R. R. Co. v . Long ,94 Ky .410 ; 2 Met .119 ;
83 Ky .129 ; 83 Ky . 675 ; in 94 Ky ., page
411 , and 92 Ky ., pages 369 , 370 , will be
found cited numerous cases on question
of excessive damages .
a(16 ) When verdict is flagrantly
against the evidence,the court can not
refuse a new trial, because party remits
part of judgment ; but if error in amount
recovered is caused by an erroneous
instruction,so much of thejudgment as
was due to such instruction , if it can be
ascertained,may be remitted and remain-
der allowed to stand . Merrick v . Holt ,
7 R. 758 .
(17) When a party undertakestopre
vent a new trialby remitting a portion
of the judgment obtained by erroneous
instructions , he is bound to remit so
much as will show the court that the
defendant can not be prejudiced by
refusing the new trial asked for, and
that the amount retained is clearly sus-
tained by the proof. Masterson v .Hagan ,
17 В. М. 325 .
(18) In an actionfor maliciousprose-
cution the plaintiffrecovered a verdict
for $4,000, the defendant filed grounds
and moved for a new trial, the judge
announced that he would grant a new
trial unless the plaintiffwould accept
$1,000; the plaintiffagreed to this, and

TITLEIX] 195NEW TRIAL .
2.Misconductof thejury, of the prevailingparty, or of his
attorney .
3.Accidentorsurprisewhich ordinaryprudencecouldnothave
guarded against.
the motion for a new trial was overruled .
The court say : " Courts have often
granted new trials on equitable condi-
tions prescribed to the applicant, but
we know no precedent for overruling a
motion for a new trialon terms required
of the party opposing it. In thisinstance
the court virtuallyassessed the damages ,
and thereby deprived each party of his
right to an assessment by the jury .
This seems to be error to the prejudice
of the defendant ,if he was entitledto a
new trial,and to the plaintiff'sprejudice
if the defendant was not entitled to a
new trial." Brown v .Morris ,3 Bush 81 ;
L. & N. R. R. v . Earl , 94 Ky .368 ; Mer-
rick v . Holt , 7 R. 758 .
(19) Grounds should be filedby order of
court. Motion and grounds for a new
trial should be filedby order of court,
entered on the order book and should
also be indorsed filed . When this is
done the motion and grounds become a
part of the record when copied into the
transcript, and it isnot necessary to in-
clude them in the bill of exceptions .
McAllister v . Ins. Co., 78 Ky . 531.
(20 ) Irregularityof judge, jury or party.
After the jury had retired for consulta-
tion,one of the witnesses, who had been
examined on the trial, was sent for by
them , admitted into the jury room and
re-examined without the knowledge of
the court or parties. Held that although
he was the witness of the party seeking
the reversal, the misconduct of the jury
authorized a new trial. Lutrell v .M. &
L. R. R. Co., 18 B. M. 291 ; and see 3 Mon.
411 .
(21) That juror was on jury that had
tried the case at a former term is not
cause for a new trial. The objection to
him should have been made before the
jury was sworn,or beforethe jury had
retired, if not discovered before that
time . Fitzpatrickv . Harris, 16 B. М.
561 .
(22) Affidavitsof jurors can not be
received to impeach their verdict for
misbehavior in themselves or their fellow
jurors. Allard v . Smith , 2 Met . 297 ; as
to when jurors will be allowed to explain
their verdict or correct mistake in it,
see Alexander v . Humber , 86 Ky . 565 ;
Johnson v . Davenport , 3 J. J. M. 396.
(23 ) An affidavitof a juror is not com-
petent evidence to show that the verdict
was the resultof an agreement that such
a verdict should be rendered as was
favored by a majority of the jury .
Lucas v . Cannon , 13 Bush 650 ; 3 Mon.
411 ; and see Cain v . Cain , 1 B. M. 214,
as to when partialityof juror is grounds
for new trial .
(24) It is a well-settled principle, ap-
plicable to the conduct of alljuries,that
the testimony of the jurors is not com-
petent to explain the grounds of their
decision, or to impeach the validity of
their findings. Com.v. Skeggs ,3 Bush 19 ;
Johnson v .Davenport , 3 J.J. M. 396.
(25) When the court rules that de-
fendant has burden of proof and requires
him to introduce his evidence first, and
afterward gives the plaintiffconclusion
of argument , defendant is entitled to a
new trial. O'Connor v .Henderson Bridge
Co., 95 Ky .693.
(26) Denial of concluding argument to
party who has burden of proof is revers-
ible error. Royal Ins. Co. v . Schwing ,
87 Ky . 410 ; Lucas v . Hunt , 91 Ky .279 .
(27 ) Where the defendant , in passing
near one of the jury during the trial,
said in his hearing , " Don't hang ," itwas
an insult to the juror, a contempt of
court and cause for a new trial. Camp-
bellv. Bannister , 79 Ky . 205.
(28) As to correspondence between the
judge and jury , after they had retired
for consultation concerning a point at is-
sue in the case, see Goode v . Campbell ,
14 Bush 75 ; as to improper statement by
judge to jury to induce them to make a
verdict, see Randolph v . Lampkin , 90
Ky . 551 .

4. Excessivedamages,appearingtohave been given under the
influenceof passionor prejudice.
5. Error in the assessmentof the amount of recovery,whether
too largeor too small,in an actionupon a contract,or forthe
injuryordetentionof property.
(29) Instructions . " Grounds which
merely state that the court erred in giv-
ing or refusing instructions is sufficient
to raise any and all questions upon this
point,but where the party expresslyin-
dicates the giving or refusal of certain
instructions as error and names none
others ,either expressly or in a general
way , he will be confined to them ."
Johns v . L. & N. R. R. , 10 R. 757 ;
Bland v .Gaither , 10 R. 1033.
(30) Where one ground for new trial
was that court erred in giving certain
designated instructions, and another
ground was that court erred in giving an
instruction as to compensatory damages ,
this latter instruction can be consid-
ered on appeal , although it was omitted
in firstground . L. & N. R. R. v .Mor-
ris,14 R. 466 .
(31) Motion for- not necessary to main-
tain appeal. Neither a motion for a new
trial nor billof exceptions is necessary
toenable the unsuccessful party to main-
tain an appeal . The only officeof a mo-
tion for a new trial and a bill of excер-
tions isto bring before the court for re-
vision matters which would not other-
wise appear . When an appeal is prose-
cuted from a judgment on a verdict, or
the judgment of the court when the law
and facts are submitted to it, without a
motion for a new trial and a bill of ex-
ceptions,nothing isbefore the court ex-
cept the pleadings , verdict and judg-
ment . Harper v . Harper , 10 Bush 447 ;
McAllister v .Ins.Co.,78 Ky .531 ; 80 Ky .
176 ; 82 Ky . 678 ; Owensboro R. R. v .
Barker ,15 R. 175 ;nor is motion for neces-
sary when a peremptory instruction has
been given . Loving v . Warren Co., 14
Bush 316 ; 2 Met .542 ; nor when plead-
ings are not sufficient to support verdict .
15 R. 175 .
(32) But " on an issue and trial of a
fact by a jury , a motion for a new trial
is essentialto correct the errorsgrowing
out of the evidence or instructions ."
Detherage v . Montgomery , 4 Bush 46 ;
Humphrey v . Walton , 2 Bush 580.
(33) Motion for- suspends judgment. A
motion for a new trial suspends the
judgment , and may be continued and
passed on at the subsequent term . But
a motion to set aside an order overruling
a motion for a new trial does not suspend
the judgment . There is no authority
for a motion to rehear a motion for a
new trial. Louisville R. & L. Co. v .
Kerr ,78 Ky .12 ; and see Harper v . Har-
per, 10 Bush 447 .
(34) Newly discovered evidence. " The
general rules governing applications for
new trialsupon the ground of newly dis-
covered evidence are : 1. That'the
names of the witnesses who have been
discovered ' must appear . 2. That the
party has been vigilant in preparing his
case for trial . 3. That the new facts
were discovered after the trial and would
be important . 4. That the evidence dis-
covered will tend to prove facts which
were not directly in issue on the trialor
were not then known , nor investigated
by the proof. 5. That the new evidence
is not merely cumulative ." Price v .
Thompson , 84 Ky . 219 .
(35 ) The affidavitof the plaintiff'sat-
torney, that he did not know of the ex-
istence of a city ordinance material as
evidence in the case until after the trial,
is not sufficient to authorize a new trial.
Babbitt v. Woolley , 3 Bush 703 ; and see
McCown v . Maklin , 7 Bush 308 ; Rhodes
v .City of Henderson , 2 R. 228 .
(36) The fact that a party does not
know of the evidence by which he can
establish his defense does not excuse him
from presenting the defense. A defense
of which the defendant must have had
knowledge , if itwas true , not reliedon,
or presented on the trial,can not be made
available on a motion for a new trial.
Mason v .Mason , 5 Bush 187.

7. Newly discoveredevidence,materialfor the party applying,

(37) Where a party discovers testimony ,
even afterthe argument has commenced ,
and it can then be given , it is his duty
to ask that itbe heard , and if he failto
offer it, it is no ground for a new trial;
nor can a party get a new trial on the
ground that he has discovered testimony
that would strengthen his case. Fleet
v. Hollenkemp , 13 B. M. 220 .
(38) Cumulative evidence is not suffi-
cient to authorize a new trial. Mason v .
Mason , 5 Bush 187 ; Allen v . Perry , 6
Bush 85 ; Bell v . Offut, 10 Bush 632.
(39 ) When the ground for a new trial
is newly discovered evidence , affidavits
must be filed to sustain it. Slone v .
Slone , 2 Met . 339 ; sec.343 .
(40) In an actionto enforcea vendor's
lien the defendant alleged want of title
and asked a rescission and the rescission
was granted; soonafterwardthe plaintiff
discovered a deed that perfected his title,
and fileda petitionfor a new trial, alleg-
ing that the index to the deed book in
which the deed was found had been lost
or destroyed , and after a diligent search
he did not discover the deed until after
the judgment ; held that he was not
bound to do more than to search for the
deed in the usual way by the aid of the
indexes , and a new trial should have
been granted . Elliottv.Harris ,81 Ky .470.
(41 ) To entitle a party to a new trial
on the grounds of newly discovered evi-
dence , it must not only have been dis-
covered too late to have been used upon
the trial , but must have been of such a
nature or so concealed that it could not
have been previously discovered by the
use of reasonable diligence. The dis-
covery of a deed on recordin the proper
office will not entitle a party to a new
trial. Denny v .Wickliffe, 1 Met . 216.
(42) The statement in the affidavitof
the witness filedin support of the motion
for a new trial that he had not com-
municated the factsto the party until
after the trial,isnot sufficient; the party
himself should state that the facts were
unknown to him until after the trial.
Bronson v .Green , 2 Duv . 234.
(43 ) Where a party losesa case because
of insufficientevidence ,he isentitledto a
new trialif prevailing party confess that
verdict isagainst justiceand truth and is
willing to so testify. Pigg v . Whitman ,
2 R. 320 .
(44) To entitlea party to a new trial,
when the point upon which it is sought
was in issue in the former trial, the dis-
covered evidence must be of such a per-
manent and unerring character as to
preponderate greatly, or have a decisive
influence upon the evidence to be over-
turned by it. Allen v .Perry , 6 Bush 85 ;
Mitchell v .Berry , 1 Met . 602 ; Mercer v .
Mercer , 87 Ky . 21 ; Porter v . First Nat .
Bank , 2 R. 212 .
(45 ) " It is a general rule that a new
trialshould not be granted upon the sole
ground of a discovery after verdict of
parol testimony concerning a point liti-
gated ,or a factknown to a party ,because
the converse of this rule would open a
wide fieldforunfairness and subornation ,
and would tend to protract ligitationand
render itnot only uncertain ,but almost
interminable ." Leonhart v . Stalzenber-
ger,7 Bush 209 ; 87 Ky .21.
(46 ) New trialon payment of costs. The
Ky . Stat ., sec. 899, provides that a new
trialmay be granted upon condition that
the costs of former trialare paid by the
party applying therefor within forty
days . But the court is vested with a
sound legal discretion in each case, and
may refuse to set aside the order grant-
ing a new trial, although the costs are
not paid within forty days . The time
should not be lessthan forty days . Myers
v .Lummis ,80 Ky .456 ; and see Mattingly
v . L. & N. R. R. , 92 Ky .463 ; Galbraith
v .Galbraith ,5 R. 859.
(47) The courtcan notorderallcoststo

§ 341[370]When notto be granted. A new trialshallnot be granted
on accountof the smallnessof damages in an actionforan injuryto
the person or reputation,nor in any otheractionin which the dam
be paid ,but only costs of former trial,

(49 ) Practice. On the calling of a case
defendant moved for a continuance on
account of absence of his counsel ; his
motion was overruled and a judgment
rendered against him , with time to pre-
pare billof exceptions . He fileda peti-
tion for a new trialon ground of cas-
ualty and misfortune ," in that his attor-
ney was absent,and the court held upon
appeal from judgment , dismissing peti-
tion, that proper remedy was by appeal
from firstjudgment and not by petition
for new trial. Beckham v . Morrison , 14
R. 241 ; when a party istaken by surprise
during the trial of a case, the correct
practice is to move for a continuance
or postponement , and not to take the
chances of a verdict,or failing in that
to apply for a new trial. Shipp v . Sug-
gett,9 B. M. 5 .
(50) The Court of Appeals may ,when
a new trialhas been granted and there is
an appeal from the second judgment ,di-
rect the entry of firstjudgment ifit has
been erroneously set aside. Couadeau v .
American Accident Co., 95 Ky . 280 ; and
see note 11 .
(51) After getting a new trial, the
party obtaining can not appeal from the
original judgment . McCall v . Hitch-
cock , 9 Bush 66 .
(52) Trialby court- motionfornew trial.
In a common law action, where the law
and facts are submitted to the court, a
motion and grounds for a new trialmade
within three days are necessary in order
to a review by the Appellate Court of
any alleged errors committed during the
trial. In the absence of a motion and
grounds for a new trial, nothing is be-
forethe court except the inquiryasto
whether the pleadings stateany cause of
action or defense , and whether the evi-
dence authorizes the judgment . Helm
v.Coffey,80 Ky .176; Henderson v.Du-
pree, 82 Ky . 678 ; Harper v . Harper , 10
Bush 447 ;and thisruleapplies,although
the conclusions of law and fact are
separately stated. 82 Ky . 678.
(53) Verdict not sustainedby law or evi-
dence. In an action to recover damages
for the loss of a slave , where the verdict
was for $ 1,500, the court say : " The
question of damages was peculiarly
within the province of the jury , and
although we might not concur with the
jury as to the correctness of their esti-
mate , we do not feel authorized to dis-
turb the verdict for that reason ." Mc-
Clain v . Esham , 17 B. M. 146 ; L. & Ν .
R. R. Co. v . Graves , 78 Ky . 74 ; Bell v.
Keach , 80 Ky .42 .
(54) Verdict willnot be disturbedbe-
cause not sustained by the evidence un-
lessit isclearlyand palpably against the
weight of it. Thompson v . Thompson .93
Ky . 435 ; Urso v .Unverzagt , 2 R. 228.
(55) If the plaintifffails to set out a
good cause of action, and the defect is
not cured by answer (Fible v .Caplinger ,
13 В. М. 464) ; or ifthe plaintiffshows no
right to sue , a verdict for the plaintiff
should be set aside. Petty v .Malier , 14
Β . Μ . 246 ; and see sec . 386 and notes
thereto as to when judgment may be
rendered against verdict.

any injury to the person special damages

§ 342. (1) Motiontobe made withinthree
days- additionalgrounds. A motion for a
new trial must be made within three

Imperial Ins.Co. v.Kiernan,83 Ky .468 ;

days nothing but pleadings and finding

posed of . Houston v . Kidwell , 83 Ky .

(5) Sunday intervening between day
verdict is rendered and motion made

(6) Trialby court. If the law and facts

classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedure for applying for a new trial when grounds are discovered after the term in which the original verdict or decision was rendered. It specifies the timeline for filing the petition, issuing a summons, and the court's decision-making process. It also explicitly states that these provisions do not apply to divorce cases concerning the judgment for divorce itself. The statute also references other sections for further provisions and limitations.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895 - Time of Trial
classification.summary: This section of the 1895 Civil and Criminal Code outlines the procedures for managing court dockets and scheduling trials. It details the creation and maintenance of three dockets: the common docket for ordinary actions, the equity docket for equity cases, and the motion docket for motions. The statutes specify how cases should be entered, arranged, and called for trial on specific days, as well as the procedures for trying issues of law in equity cases.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details the procedural aspects of managing court dockets and scheduling trials. It describes the organization and function of the common docket, equity docket, and motion docket. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1895. The confidence is high because the content is purely procedural and lacks any racial indicators.

It covers topics such as the definition of a judgment, judgments involving multiple parties, dismissal of actions, set-offs and counter-claims, enforcement of liens, foreclosure of mortgages, and the set-off of judgments for money. The text also includes references to case law and other statutory sections.

(10) Nunc pro tunc judgment. Although
neither the minutes nor order book
showed that judgment had been ren-
dered at a previous term, the filingby
defendants of supersedeas bond and as-
signment of errors were sufficientadmis-
sions of record to authorize the court to
enter a nunc pro tunc judgment . Wade
v . Bryant , 9 R. 875 .
(11) " During the term the court has
power to alter or amend the record ac-
cording to the truth of the case, but
after the term expires the court ceases
to have such power , except in cases of
clericalmisprision,and even then it isan
inviolable rule that no amendment can
be made unless there is something in the

and see Raymond v . Smith , 1 Met . 65 ,

(14) Res judicata. The burden is upon
party relying upon the bar to show that
the merits of the case were considered .
Pepper v . Donnelly , 87 Ky .259.
(15) The rule is well settled that to
constitutethe former judgment a bar to
another suit founded on the same cause of
action it must appear to have been a
decision upon the merits. But if the
trial went off on a technical defect , or
because the debt was not due ,or because
the court had not jurisdiction, or be-
cause of the temporary disabilityof the
plaintiffto sue,or the like,the judgment

missed at defendant's costs , and leave

ing a copy in the papers ," is not a final

(17 ) When demurrer to petitionisover-
ruled and case heard upon law and facts
and petitiondismissed ,itwill be presumed
in the absence of rebutting circumstances
that the trialwas upon the merits, but
the presumption is not conclusive. 87
Ky .259 ; see further, notes to sec.371.
(18) Judgment dismissingpetitionisa
bar to another action fo rthe same relief,
provided the determinationhas reached
the merits of the case, and whether the
factsupon which court acts are shown by
evidence or averred in petition and ad.

ant for the same ,or a different asporta-

classification.title: Dismissal of action without prejudice.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions under which a civil or criminal action can be dismissed without prejudice, meaning it can be refiled later. Dismissal can be initiated by the plaintiff before the final submission of the case, or by the court for reasons such as the plaintiff's failure to appear, lack of necessary parties, failure to prosecute diligently, or disobedience of court orders. The statute also clarifies that certain dismissals do not bar future actions, such as those based on technical defects, jurisdictional issues, or temporary plaintiff disabilities.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the procedural rules for dismissing legal actions in Kentucky. It outlines the circumstances under which a case can be dismissed without prejudice, allowing for refiling. There is no mention of race, ethnicity, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it appears to be part of a compiled legal code from 1895. The confidence is high because the statute's content is purely procedural and lacks any racial implications. No human review is needed.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895, Section 372
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895, specifically § 372, deals with the trial of set-offs or counter-claims. It establishes that a defendant is entitled to a trial on a set-off or counter-claim even if the plaintiff dismisses their action or fails to appear. The text also includes several case law annotations related to the dismissal of actions, filing away cases, and the rights of parties in various legal proceedings. None of the content within this section pertains to racial segregation or discrimination.

The content is purely procedural and relates to the conduct of civil litigation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines general rules for judgments in legal proceedings. It covers scenarios involving multiple defendants, the enforcement of liens and mortgages, and the distinction between foreclosure and other forms of judgment. Specifically, it addresses when judgments can be rendered against only a portion of defendants, the immediate sale of property to enforce liens, the prohibition of foreclosure, and the ability to obtain both a sale of property and a personal judgment against a defendant. It also includes case law citations to clarify these points.

Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Infant may have new trial - when.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions under which an infant, excluding a married woman, can seek a new trial for a judgment. The infant has twelve months after reaching the age of twenty-one to show cause against a judgment, unless the judgment was for a tort committed by the infant, necessaries furnished to the infant, or rendered upon a set-off or counter-claim. The vacation of such a judgment will not affect the title of a bona fide purchaser under it.

The document type is 'codes' as it is presented as part of a compiled code (Civil and Criminal Code 1895).

§ 414. (1) Appeal by- effectof. A de-
fendant , constructively summoned , may
prosecute an appeal and have the judg-
ment reversed,but if he electsto appeal
he is bound by the judgment of the
lower court as much as if he had been
served with process, and is precluded
from having a new trialas provided in
thissection. Salterv .Dunn ,1 Bush 311.
A motion must be made to set aside
judgment before an appeal can be prose-
cuted , sec . 763 .
(2 ) The right to have a retrialwithin
fiveyears does not divest a non -resident
of the right to appeal , and obtain a re-
versal for errorsapparent in the record .
If the judgment is apparently regular
and proper ,but was unjustly obtained ,
the proper remedy is by motion in the
Circuit Court . Payne v . Witherspoon ,
14 В. М. 270 ; 1 Bush 466. But see sec.
763 , and notes thereto.
(3 ) " Defendants , constructively sum-
moned , have alternateremedies to retry
the cause : First, by proper proceedings
in the court below , second , by an ap-
peal to this court, but not by both rem-
edies ,and in the two courts ,at the same
time ." Speak v . Mattingly , 4 Bush 310.
(4) New trial- how obtained. A defend-
ant is not entitled to a new trial until he
moves to have the action retried, gives
bond for costs and presents a defense ;
and it is only after his defense is sus-
tained, in whole or in part, that the
court has power to set aside the judg-
ment . Williams v.Taylor,11 Bush 375.
(5 ) Although the defendants did not
make a formal motion to have the action
retried, they gave security for costs, and
filedtheirpleadingsof record,and these
steps are sufficientto enter their appear-
ance and motion . Barbee v .Fox ,79 Ky .
588 .
(6) Rights of when judgment modified.
When a sale of a non -resident defend-
ant's property has been made under a
judgment , he may , upon succeeding in
even modifying the decree , elect to va-
cate the sale ; and he has the same right
when he takes an appeal and the judg-
ment against him is reversed . Jackson
v .Speed , 2 Duv . 426 ; Lusk v . Salter, 1
Bush 311 .
(7 ) Sale- exceptionsto. Where a non-
resident appeared and moved for a re-
trialmore than fiveyears after the sale
of the property in an action to foreclose
a mortgage , but within fiveyears after
the sale was confirmed , the sale may be
setaside; and when exceptions are filed,
the non -resident has the right to avail
himself of every objection that could
have been made if he had been actually
served with process. 79 Ky . 588 ; 11
Bush 375 .

classification.title: Actions Against Absent and Unknown Defendants
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines procedures for actions against absent or unknown defendants. It details how such defendants can be admitted to make a defense, the retrial process, and the restoration of money or property. It also specifies how the title of property sold under attachment or judgment is affected by a new trial, and clarifies that an attorney for the plaintiff is not considered a good faith purchaser if they buy the defendant's property at a sale. The statute also notes that this section does not apply to divorce judgments concerning the divorce itself.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text deals with legal procedures for handling cases where defendants are absent or unknown. It outlines the rights of such defendants to a retrial and the conditions under which property can be restored. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The statute appears to be a procedural rule of general applicability within the legal system. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

§ 418. (1) Attachment necessaryto create
lien- when . In an action to subject real
estate owned by a defendant construct-
ively summoned , the levy of an attach-

classification.title: Settlement of Trust and Decedent's Estates
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines procedures for the settlement of trust and decedent's estates. It details who may institute an action, necessary parties, averments of the petition, sale of real estate, reference to a commissioner, notice to creditors, and the effect of creditors failing to present their claims. It also covers legatees and distributees' liability to creditors and the requirement of refunding bonds.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895, specifically dealing with the settlement of estates. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. The content is purely procedural regarding property and debt settlement. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Settlement of Trust and Decedent's Estates
classification.summary: This statute outlines the legal process for settling the estates of deceased persons. It details who can initiate an action for settlement, the necessary parties to include in such an action (representatives, lienholders, interested parties, and creditors), and the required averments in the petition, including the debts and property of the decedent. It also specifies the court's power to order the sale of real estate if the personal estate is insufficient to cover debts and the procedure for notifying creditors to appear before a commissioner.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the legal procedures for settling the estates of deceased individuals. It covers aspects like who can bring an action, who must be a party, and how debts are to be paid, including the potential sale of real estate. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it appears to be part of a compiled legal code from 1895.

a creditor is made defendant to a suit to
settlean estate,and served with process,
he should be compelled to either set up
or abandon his claim ; and ,if he failsto
present it, its recovery against the ad-

(3) Order of reference. As the Code im-
peratively requires an order of reference
to be made in all cases for the settlement

(5) Practiceregardingpresentingof claims.
The usual practice in actions for the set-

is for creditors to present their claims ,
verifiedand proved as required by
statute. Upon exceptions to the master's
report, the sufficiencyof the verification
and proof can always be inquired into;
and , in such cases, it seems allowable to
investigate and pass upon the merits of
the claim. But a creditorhas a right
to filehis petition setting up his claim ,
and , when itis controverted, he has the
right to demand that the grounds of de-
fense be stated, according to the rules of
pleading , in the form of a verified an-
swer , or by exception containing the
denials or allegationsnecessary to present
a defense. Horner v . Harris , 10 Bush
357. See Francis v . Smith , 1 Duv .
121 .

fore commissioner becomes a party to

fant creditor being made a party to a

judgment of distribution is binding on

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines procedures for the settlement of trust and decedent's estates. It details requirements for creditors to present claims against an estate, including the necessity of an affidavit stating no offset or discount against the claim. It also specifies how accounts and written debts should be proven and discusses exceptions, such as the Commonwealth not needing to verify claims. It also clarifies that these proceedings do not bar other actions like trespass or waste.

It defines forcible detainer as the refusal of a tenant to give possession to their landlord after the lease term expires, or in cases of tenants at will or by sufferance after the determination of their tenancy. It also covers situations where a person who has made a forcible entry refuses to yield possession to the rightful possessor or landlord.

Case law annotations clarify that the focus is on actual possession, not title, and that a tenant in possession is the proper party to bring the action.

Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Settlements of fiduciaries - recording of.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for settling the accounts of committees and trustees, stating that these settlements have the same effect as those prescribed by section four hundred and seventy-one. It specifies that the county court of the county where the committee is appointed or the trust deed/will is recorded has jurisdiction. It also details that settlements of personal representatives, trustees, guardians, or other fiduciaries made before a commissioner or receiver, and confirmed by the court, shall be recorded by the clerk in a designated book. Vouchers accompanying these settlements are not to be recorded. The clerk is entitled to fees similar to those for county court clerks, and the commissioner or receiver must state the recording fees on the settlement or report.

The statute appears to be a procedural matter within civil law concerning estate and trust management. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

§ 474. (1) Definitionof mandamus . The
writ of mandamus is not now , as former-
ly, a high prerogative writ, emanating
from the grace or favor of any one , but
it is a statutory writ or order of a court
of competent jurisdiction,and in a prop-
er case is to be granted on the motion of
any partyaggrieved. Maddox v.Graham ,
2 Met . 56 .
(2 ) We have no other than peremptory
writs of mandamus ; it can not be in the
alternative . 10 Bush 564 .
(3) Defenseagainst. In a proceedingby
mandamus , a counter-claim to recover
back money paid on the contract is not
proper ; but if the defendant state suffi-
cient reasonsfor not paying the claim,
the writ will not be awarded . 18 В. М.
848 .
(4 ) Discretionnot controlled. The per-
formance of a plain, positive duty may
be compelled by mandamus , but where
there is a discretion as to the result that
may be arrived at, it can not be con-
trolled . If an inferior tribunal has a
discretion, and proceeds to exercise it,
itsdiscretioncan not be controlledby
mandamus , but if it refuses to entertain
a question which the law has enjoined
upon its consideration, mandamus will
lie. A mandamus may be issued against
a county court to require it to consider
the proprietyof buildinga bridge on a
county road,under sec.29,art.1,chap .
94,Gen. Stat.;but when ithas consid-
ered it,and refusedto build,itcan not
be compelled to do so. Com . v .Boone
Co. , 82 Ky .632 ; see Nelson Co. v . Wash-
ington Co. ,14 B. M. 92 ; Dickens v .Cave
Hill,93 Ky .385.
(5) Mandamus will not lie to control
the exercise of a judicial discretion.
City v . Kean , 18 B. M. 9 ; Goheen v .
Myers, 18 B. M. 423; Gayle v. Owen
Co., 83 Ky . 61 ; City v. Berry, 80
Ky . 354; Ohio Co. v. Newton , 79 Ky.
267; Wright v.Baker ,94 Ky .343.
(6) Mandamus liesto compel an in-
feriorjudicial tribunal to act,but not to
controlitsjudgment . Cassidyv.Young ,
92 Ky . 227 ;to set a court in motion but
not to control itsdecision. Shine v.Ky .
Central R. R. ,85 Ky . 177 .
(7) County courtwillnot be compelled

(11) Legalduty to performactmust ap.
pear. It must appear upon every ap-
plication for a mandamus that it is the
legal duty of the respondent to do that
which it issought to compel him to do ,
and that upon proper application he re-
fused to perform that duty . Lowe v .
Phelps , 14 Bush 642 ; Norman v . Board
of Mgrs .,93 Ky .537.
(12 ) Mandamus - when granted. Manda-
mus isthe proper remedy to prevent the
clerk and judge of the county court
from recording the vote under a " local
option " law , if the law is unconstitu-
tional. Gayle v.Owen Co.,83 Ky .61.
(13 ) And to compel the clerk of the
county court to record a deed or other
instrument which it is made his duty to
record. Wulftange v . McCollom , 83 Ky .
361 .
(14) And to require the county court
to show cause why it did not permit a
deputy sheriffto qualify. Applegate v .
Applegate , 4 Met . 236 ; see Prater v .
Strother, 11 R. 831.
(15) And to compel the county judge
to accept the bond of a sheriffand per-
mit him to qualify. Cate v . Ross , 2
Duv .243 ; and to compel the State Board
of Pharmacy to enter a " graduate in
pharmacy ." State Board v. White , 84
Ky . 626.
(16 ) And to compel county court to
levy a tax to pay a medical account for
services rendered to a poor person by re-
questof the county judge and justices.
Rodman v . Larue Co. , 3 Bush 144.
(17) And to compel county court to
levy a tax to pay for a bridge built, un-
der a contract with the county . Ander-
son Co. v . Stone , 18 B. Μ . 848 .
(18 ) And to compel examining board
to compare poll-books to perform their
duty. Clark v. McKenzie ,7 Bush 523;
Batman v .Megowan , 1 Met . 533 ; Howes
v . Walker , 92 Ky . 258 ; but mandamus
will not lie to compel the canvassing
board to recanvass the ballots on account
of error in the firstcanvass ,as the party
aggrieved by the former canvass has a
remedy by contestingtheelection. Hous-
ton v . Steele, 16 R.-
(19 ) And to require trusteesof a town
to repair the streets. Hammar v .City , 3
Met . 494 ; Trustees v . Kinner , 12 Bush
334 .

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 details the procedures for obtaining writs of mandamus and prohibition. It outlines the filing of petitions, the response of the opposing party (demurrer or answer), and the court's role in hearing and deciding the motion. It also includes case law annotations related to the application of these writs in various legal contexts, such as probate, justice of the peace actions, injunctions, interpleaders, and compelling public officials to perform their duties. Specific examples include compelling the recording of votes under a 'local option' law, recording deeds, allowing deputy sheriffs to qualify, accepting sheriff's bonds, and levying taxes for medical accounts or bridges. It also covers compelling examining boards to compare poll-books and trustees to repair streets.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details legal procedures for writs of mandamus and prohibition. It outlines how these legal mechanisms are used to compel or restrain actions by public officials and courts. There is no mention of race, and the procedures described are general legal processes applicable to all citizens. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of statutes.

§ 476[525]Temporarypreventiveordersmay bemade. During thependency
of the motion,the courtorjudge,in vacation,may make temporary
ordersfor preventingdamage or injuryto the applicantuntilthe

§ 477[526]Mandamusdefined. The writ of mandamus ,as treatedof in
thischapter,isan orderof a courtof competent and originaljuris-
diction, commanding an executiveor ministerialofficerto perform
an act,or omit to do an act,the performance or omissionof which is
enjoinedby law; and isgranted on the motion ofthepartyaggrieved,
oroftheCommonwealth when thepublicinterestisaffected.
(20 ) And against auditor, to compel
him to pay claims . Auditor v . Adams ,

490 ; 14 Bush 284 ; 3 Bush 231; 80 Ку .
336; 78 Ky .577; 8 Bush 98.
(21) Corporation may be compelled by
mandamus to elect officers . Orr v .
Bracken Co., 81 Ky .593 .
(22) Where county is divided and a
new county established, the old county
may by mandamus compel the new to
levy a tax to pay indebtedness created
before division. Montgomery Co. v .
Menefee Co.,93 Ky .33 .
(23 ) When a city council is authorized
and required to levy and collect a tax to
pay the interest on bonds , a writ of
mandamus may be maintained by any
of the bondholders to compel it to dis-
charge that duty . Maddox v . Graham ,
2 Met . 56 .
(24 ) A county bondholder may compel
by mandamus the county court to levy
and collecta tax to pay bonds issued by
the county . Elliott Co. v . Kitchen , 14
Bush 289. And a corporation may have
the writ to compel the county to issue
bonds in compliance with a subscription.
C. & O. R. R. Co. v . Washington Co. , 10
Bush 564 .
(25 ) Mandamus - when not granted. A
person who is not eligibleto hold office
can not have a mandamus to compel the
county judge to permit him to qualify,
although he received a majority of the
votes cast at the election . Atchinson v .
Lucas , 83 Ky .451 ; and see notes 4-8.
(26 ) Nor willitlieagainst officersof a
private corporation, Cook v . College, 9
Bush 541 ; nor to compel the officersof a
benefit society to restore an expelled
member . Schmidt v . Lodge ,84 Ky .490 .
(27) Nor will mandamus lie where
party has any other adequate remedy ,
such as right of appeal. Shine v . Ky .
Central R. R. , 85 Ky . 177 .
(28 ) An ex parte allowance made by
the circuit court to guards summoned
by its order to guard the jail is only
prima facie evidence of its correctness,
and , when controverted by the county
court, mandamus will not lieto compel
the county court to levy a tax to pay
the allowance . Justice's v . Moore , 2
Bush 108 .
(29) Nor isitthe proper remedy when
the amount of the demand is not ascer-
tained. Garrard Co. v . McKee , 11 Bush
234 .
(30) Motion for. Failure to make the
motion for a mandamus on the day
specifiedis a waiver of the right to en-
ter or make it under that notice . 14
Bush 289 .
(31 ) Partiestoaction.Mandamus against
county court to compel it to levy a tax
must be against persons composing the
court. Montgomery Co. v . Menefee Co. ,
93 Ky . 33 .
(32) Power of CourtofAppealsto issue
writ ismentioned in Preston v . Fidelity
Trust Co. ,94 Ky .295, but not decided ;
and see Rohmeiser v . Bannon , 15 R. 114.
In Kelley v . Toney , 95 Ky . 338, and
Louisville School of Reform v .City , 88
Ky . 584, itisheld that mandamus from
Court of Appeals is proper remedy to
compel lower court to grant an appeal
which has been refused without right;
and see Vance v . Field, 89 Ky .178 .(17)

classification.title: WRITS OF MANDAMUS AND PROHIBITION
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines the procedures and definitions for writs of mandamus and prohibition. It details when temporary preventive orders can be made, defines a writ of mandamus as a court order commanding an executive or ministerial officer to perform or omit an act enjoined by law, and provides numerous case examples illustrating its application in compelling various public and corporate entities to fulfill their legal duties, such as paying claims, electing officers, levying taxes, and issuing bonds. It also specifies conditions under which a writ of mandamus will not be granted, including when a party is ineligible for office, when the remedy is against officers of a private corporation, when another adequate remedy exists, or when the amount of the demand is not ascertained. The text also touches upon the motion process and parties involved in mandamus actions, as well as the power of the Court of Appeals to issue such writs.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details legal procedures related to writs of mandamus and prohibition. It focuses on the mechanics of court orders compelling public officials and entities to perform their legal duties. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled Civil and Criminal Code.

§ 479. (1) Prohibition. Court of Ap-
pealswillnot grant a writof prohibition.
Sasseen v . Hammond , 18 B. M. 672 ; but

(2 ) When a matter iswithin the juris-

Bank Lick Co. v. Phelps , 81 Ky . 613 .
(3) When by the provisions of a city

forcement of an ordinance ,the ordinance

ute , the writ of prohibition is the proper

Pennington v . Woolfolk ,79 Ky .13 .
(5) A writ of prohibition can only be

to a legislativebody ,such as a citycoun-
cil. Patton v . Stephens , 14 Bush 324.
(6) After dismissing an appeal for
want of jurisdiction, the circuit court
properly refused a writ of prohibition

by the justicewho rendered it. Olmstead

(7 ) A temporary order prohibiting a
court of inferior jurisdiction from pro-
ceeding in a certain case pending the
hearing of a writ of prohibition does not
continue in force after writ is refused,
although the judgment refusing writ is
superseded . Gibbs v . Board of Alder-
men , 95 Ky . 471.
(8) A writ of prohibitionwillnot be
granted where the inferior court sought
to be restrainedhas jurisdiction. Gold-
smith v . Owen , 95 Ky . 420.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895, specifically sections 478 and 479, deals with legal procedures related to writs of mandamus and prohibition. Section 478 allows the Commonwealth to appeal without security in cases where its interest is affected by a writ of mandamus. Section 479 defines the writ of prohibition as an order from a circuit court to an inferior court to prevent it from acting outside its jurisdiction. The accompanying notes provide case law interpretations and applications of these legal writs.
classification.reasoning: The provided text from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 discusses legal procedures concerning writs of mandamus and prohibition. These statutes outline the process for appeals and the definition and application of these legal orders. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895 - Section 490: Sale of Jointly Owned Real Estate
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines the procedures for selling real estate that is jointly owned by two or more individuals. It specifies conditions under which such sales can be ordered by a court of equity, including situations where one or more owners are of unsound mind or are infants. The statute also details the application of sale proceeds, venue for actions, and conditions for voiding sales, particularly concerning infants' property. It addresses situations involving married women, contingent interests, and creditors of joint owners.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text pertains to the legal procedures for selling jointly owned real estate, including provisions for owners who are infants or of unsound mind. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The statute is purely procedural and civil in nature, dealing with property law. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It details the parties who must be made defendants in such actions, including guardians, committees, and owners of reversion or remainder interests. The statute also specifies conditions under which sales are permissible, such as for investment in other real property, and conditions under which they are forbidden, such as by deed, will, or contract. It also addresses the sale of a wife's land and the necessity of a decree to pass title to an infant's real estate.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines procedures for the sale of real property belonging to persons under disability. It details the role of court-appointed commissioners in handling funds from sales, requiring reinvestment in real estate or other legally permissible properties. The statute also specifies that deeds should be conveyed without warranty and that the court must approve the conveyance. It further addresses the investment of proceeds from sales authorized by section four hundred and eighty-nine, allowing for investment in real estate, U.S. or state bonds, or bank stock, subject to the same trusts and limitations as the original estates. Provisions are also made for the preservation and disbursement of these proceeds.

It outlines the roles of court commissioners, the process of conveying property, and the management of sale proceeds. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is presented as part of a compiled code from 1895.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895 - Title X, Chapter 113, Sections 495-496
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines procedures for the sale of real property, including provisions for the descent of proceeds, division of property, consent of trustees, and compensation for dower rights. It details how proceeds are handled if the owner dies during infancy, is of unsound mind, or is a married woman who hasn't received proceeds. It also addresses situations where a defendant objects to a sale and allows for property division. The statute specifies when a trustee's consent is necessary for a sale and outlines how dower rights are handled during property sales, including compensation from proceeds. It also notes exceptions for bonds and privy examinations in certain cases and allows for the sale of a joint owner's share if its value is less than $100, even if other owners do not consent.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text deals with the legal procedures for the sale of real property, including the distribution of proceeds, dower rights, and situations involving minors or individuals of unsound mind. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The statute appears to be a general civil procedure law. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895
classification.summary: This section of the 1895 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines procedures for the sale of real property involving infants and persons of unsound mind. It details how purchase money for their shares should be handled, remaining a lien on the land with interest until certain conditions are met (infant reaching majority, person of unsound mind becoming sound, or guardian/committee executing a bond). It also addresses the receipt of proceeds by married women, specifying it as their separate property, and allows courts to permit guardians, committees, or married women to receive interest without executing a bond. Another section discusses the joinder of parties in actions concerning jointly owned property. Finally, it addresses trust estates with remainders, allowing circuit courts to direct trustees to sell or mortgage land under specific conditions, provided all parties with vested interests are included in the action.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The language used is purely legal and procedural, focusing on the rights and protections of vulnerable individuals and the administration of property. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This statute outlines the legal procedures for the sale or mortgage of real property involving individuals with vested or contingent interests, or those who could take under limitations or provisions of a deed. It requires court approval, demonstrating that the sale or mortgage would be beneficial to all parties. The proceeds are to be paid into court and reinvested or used for permanent improvements on the mortgaged land, with provisions for payment of costs, taxes, and the management of rents by a life-tenant, including the potential appointment of a receiver.

It focuses on procedural requirements for court approval and the management of proceeds to ensure benefits for all parties involved. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The language used is general legal terminology applicable to property law. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: PROCEEDINGS TO REVERSE AND VACATE JUDGMENTS
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines procedures for reversing and vacating judgments. It details specific instances where judgments may be considered void or subject to correction, such as judgments against infants or persons of unsound mind without proper defense, errors in computation of interest or fees, omissions in crediting payments, failures to include all parties or notes in default judgments, and procedural errors in rendering judgments before an action stands for trial or before proper service of process. It also distinguishes between clerical misprisions (correctable errors) and judicial errors (errors of law).
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses legal procedures for reversing and vacating judgments. It details various technical errors and omissions that could lead to a judgment being overturned, such as issues with service of process, failure to include all parties, or errors in calculation. There is no mention of race, ethnicity, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1895.

classification.title: Modification or vacation of judgment after term

These conditions include granting a new trial for specific causes, correcting clerical errors, or vacating judgments obtained through fraud, mistake, or unavoidable casualty and misfortune. It also details procedures for vacating judgments obtained against infants or persons of unsound mind.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895 - Proceedings to Reverse and Vacate Judgments
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines the grounds and procedures for reversing or vacating judgments. classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895 - Depositions
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines the rules and conditions for using depositions in legal actions. It specifies when proof must be made by depositions or exhibits, the conditions under which a deposition can be retaken, and the circumstances under which a witness's deposition may be read in court (e.g., if the witness resides far away, is absent from the state, holds certain public offices, is a professional, is deceased, is of unsound mind, is imprisoned, or is in military service). It also addresses when a witness is compelled to attend court for oral examination and when the court may order a witness's personal attendance despite exemptions.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the procedural rules for using depositions in legal proceedings. It covers when depositions are required, when they can be retaken, and the various conditions under which a deposition can be read in court instead of requiring the witness's personal appearance. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The rules apply generally to all litigants and witnesses. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895 - Officers Authorized to Take Depositions
classification.summary: This section of the 1895 Civil and Criminal Code outlines the procedures and authorized officers for taking depositions in legal proceedings. It specifies when parties can begin taking depositions, the conditions under which depositions can be used, and designates examiners appointed by circuit judges as the primary officers for this task. It also details where examiners' offices should be located and their authority to administer oaths. The statute lists other officers, including judges, clerks, justices of the peace, and notaries public, who can also take depositions within the state. It clarifies that officers are not required to leave their offices to take depositions but can do so within their appointed county.

It specifies who can take depositions, where they can be taken, and the conditions for their use. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it does not appear to be a Jim Crow law. The document type is identified as 'codes' because it is part of a compiled Civil and Criminal Code from 1895.

It also includes case law regarding when depositions can be read against parties and the rights of adverse parties to read depositions filed but not used by the opposing party.

sustained,the court should have granted
a continuance. Moore v.Smith ,88 Ky .

inal Code .

(4 ) Impeachmentof witness. The ex-
tent of the inquiry allowable on the ex-
amination in chief is as to the general
character of the witness, whether good
or bad ; his or her general character for
truth or veracity. The general moral
character of the witness is also a fair
subject of inquiry; but not general char-
acter as to any particular acts of igno-
miny or turpitude . The particular facts
can be inquired into by the other side,
and such inquiry, when begun on cross-
examination , may be pursued by the
party impeaching ; but he has no right,
on the examination in chief, to inquire
into particular facts, or general reputa-
tion as to such facts . Thurman v . Vir-
gin , 18 B. M. 785;79 Ky .205 ; and a wit-
ness may be impeached without refer-
ence to the materiality of his testimony .
Davis v .Com .,95 Ky . 19.
(5 ) " The regular mode of inquiring
into the reputation of a witness isto ask
of those introduced for that purpose
whether they know the general reputa-
tion of the person in question among his
neighbors , and what that reputation is.
The inquiry must be as to his general
reputation where he is best known , or
with those among whom he dwells. And
when the impeaching witness is asked
for what others say respecting such per-
son , he must be able to state what is
generally said by those among whom he
dwells or with whom he is conversant .
But itis not essentialto the admissibility
of such statements that they should
come from those who are both neighbors
and personal acquaintances of the party
impeached . They are admissible if made
either by those who are acquainted with

diate neighborhood ." Henderson
Hayne , 2 Met . 342. See Campbell v .

475 .

fiesto a material fact prejudicial to the

ments touching this fact. Wrenn v . L.,
St. L. & T. R. R. , 14 R. 324 ; and see as

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines general rules of examination for witnesses in legal proceedings. It details how a witness's credibility can be challenged, including through evidence of prior convictions for felony, statements made by the witness, general character for truth and veracity, and any interest the witness may have in the controversy. It also covers the privilege of a witness not to answer questions that could expose them to criminal charges, while still allowing examination into facts that might disgrace or degrade them for the purpose of impairing credibility. The text also specifies the time frame for evidence of character in impeachment proceedings.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895, Title XIII, General Rules of Examination
classification.summary: This section of the 1895 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines general rules for the examination of witnesses in legal proceedings. It covers how to impeach a witness by prior inconsistent statements, the admissibility of evidence of good character, re-examination of witnesses, and the separation of witnesses from the courtroom during trials. It also includes case law annotations related to these rules.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details general rules of evidence and witness examination within the legal system. It does not contain any language or provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race, nor does it establish separate facilities or differential treatment for different racial groups. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines general rules of examination for witnesses in legal proceedings. It covers compelling testimony from individuals present in court, the examination of judges and jurors, and the rules for introducing handwriting samples for comparison to prove genuineness. The provisions regarding handwriting comparison include requirements for proving genuineness, establishing the time of writing, providing notice to the opposing party, and limiting the number of samples.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The rules are procedural and apply universally to all parties in legal proceedings. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Competency of Witnesses Generally
classification.summary: This section outlines the general rules for witness competency in legal proceedings, stating that any person is competent to testify unless found incapable of understanding the facts by the court. It also lists specific exceptions and modifications, such as provisions for husbands and wives, infants, lunatics, dead persons, parties against constructively summoned defendants, attorneys and priests regarding communications, assignors of claims, and the ability of adverse parties to be compelled to testify. It also notes that religious belief does not affect competency and lists crimes that disqualify a witness, such as perjury.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text deals with the general competency of witnesses in legal proceedings. It outlines who is eligible to testify and under what conditions, including exceptions related to marital status, mental capacity, and criminal convictions. There is no mention of race, and the provisions apply universally to all individuals. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is identified as 'codes' because it is part of a compiled legal code (Civil and Criminal Code 1895).

classification.title: Husband and wife- exceptions.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the competency of husbands and wives as witnesses in legal proceedings. It generally prohibits them from testifying about communications made during marriage or against each other. Exceptions are made for actions concerning lost baggage, actions that could have been brought by or against the wife if unmarried, and in specific circumstances where one spouse may testify for the other. The statute also includes provisions regarding attorneys as witnesses and the admissibility of entries in books.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the rules of evidence concerning testimony by spouses and attorneys, and the admissibility of book entries. It does not contain any language or provisions that suggest racial discrimination or segregation, which are characteristic of Jim Crow laws. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a confidence of 1.0. The category is null as there is no Jim Crow dimension. The document type is 'codes' as it is presented as part of a compiled legal code (Civil and Criminal Code 1895).

classification.title: COMPETENCY OF WITNESSES
classification.summary: This statute outlines the rules for the competency of witnesses in legal proceedings, particularly concerning testimony about verbal statements, transactions, or acts involving infants, individuals of unsound mind, or deceased persons. It specifies conditions under which such testimony is admissible, often requiring that the opposing party or their representative was present and of sound mind when the event occurred, or that certain exceptions are met (e.g., the infant or their guardian has testified, or the person of unsound mind has testified when of sound mind). It also includes specific rules regarding agents, assignees, and devisees in relation to testimony about transactions with deceased individuals or their estates.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses the competency of witnesses in legal proceedings, focusing on rules related to testimony about individuals who are infants, of unsound mind, or deceased. It details exceptions and conditions for admitting such testimony. There is no mention of race, and the rules appear to apply generally to all individuals regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code (Civil and Criminal Code 1895).

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895 - Competency of Witnesses
classification.summary: This section of the 1895 Civil and Criminal Code outlines rules regarding the competency of witnesses in legal proceedings. It details restrictions on testifying for oneself against a constructively summoned party, limits on introducing testimony after other testimony has been presented, rules for attorney-client and priest-confessor privilege, and conditions under which a party can testify against a deceased or lunatic person. It also includes general rules about party competency and specific case examples related to testimony in various legal situations, such as against decedents or in partnership matters.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text deals with general rules of evidence and witness competency in legal proceedings. It does not contain any language or provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race, nor does it relate to the typical categories of Jim Crow laws such as segregation, disenfranchisement, or differential treatment of racial groups. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Security for Costs
classification.summary: This chapter outlines requirements for plaintiffs to provide security for costs in legal actions. Specifically, non-residents and corporations (except banks) must file a bond approved by the clerk to cover potential court costs. If such a bond is not provided, the action can be dismissed upon the defendant's motion. The statute also addresses situations where a plaintiff becomes a non-resident during an action and when additional security may be required. Attorneys are liable until the bond is given.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from Chapter I of Title XIV of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895, titled 'Security for Costs'. The statutes (§§ 616-621) detail the procedural requirements for plaintiffs to post a bond to cover legal costs. These requirements apply to non-residents and corporations, with specific exceptions for banks. There is no mention of race or any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it appears to be part of a compiled legal code. The confidence is high because the text clearly defines procedural rules unrelated to race. The category is null as it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Security for Costs
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines the requirements for plaintiffs to provide security for costs in legal actions. It details when a plaintiff, including those who become non-residents after the action begins, or those suing on behalf of individuals under disability (like infants), or assignees, must provide a bond for costs. The code also addresses situations where additional security may be required and the liability of attorneys if security is not provided. It includes provisions for poor infants to prosecute actions without security and clarifies attorney liability based on their role (plaintiff vs. defendant).

The rules apply generally to all plaintiffs and their representatives, regardless of their racial background. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It details how parties can agree on a case, the record of such an agreement, and the process for judgment and appeal. It also covers offers made by defendants regarding the amount of recovery in civil actions, including the effect of accepting or rejecting such offers on costs.

classification.title: Offer to Confess Judgment
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedure for a defendant to offer to confess judgment for a portion of a claim in a civil action for the recovery of money. If the plaintiff refuses the offer and does not recover more than was offered, the plaintiff must pay the defendant's costs incurred after the offer. The offer itself cannot be used as evidence of the cause of action or the amount due.

Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it appears to be part of a compiled legal code from 1895.

The statute also details the claimant's or purchaser's recourse to the bond and the disposition of surplus sale proceeds.

(8) Officer- when bond protectionto. To

It details the conditions under which an officer can demand such a bond, the liabilities of the obligors, and the protection afforded to the officer. The text specifies that the bond is to indemnify the officer against damages from seizure or sale, and to compensate any claimant or purchaser. It also discusses the timing of bond execution and return for officer protection.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895, Section 342
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines procedures for the sale of real property to satisfy liens. It details how to handle situations where the entire property must be sold, even if it brings more than the required sum, and how to distribute the proceeds. It also addresses the rights of other lienholders and the order of sale when multiple debts are secured by the same lien or by liens of equal rank. Specific provisions are made for cases where debts are not all due at the commencement of the action or before judgment.

Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

(18) Although the judgment did not

offerto selllessthan the entire property

from the court -house . The sale having

rection of and in the presence of the

(23) In making salesthe commissioner

touching matters that the judgment does

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895, page 349
classification.summary: This section of the 1895 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky details provisions related to the conduct of sales, particularly concerning the duties and discretion of commissioners. It covers aspects like the time for executing bonds, accepting bids, reselling property due to mistakes, selling property subject to dower rights, offering to sell less than the entire property if divisible, following judgment directions for advertising and sale location, and the use of auctioneers. It also touches upon the necessity and process of property appraisement before sales for debt payment, and the qualifications and duties of appraisers.

(26 ) Inadequacy of price. An advance

(27 ) A resale should not be ordered ,
nor should the bidding be opened , upon
an increased offer of ten per cent . A
sale will not be disturbed for mere in-
adequacy of price, unless there has been
such a sacrificeof the property as to im-
port fraud . There must be either fraud
or misconduct in some one connected
with the sale,some surpriseor misappre-
hension on the part of those interested,
or of the officerwho conducts the sale,
or some irregularityin the proceedings,
or other circumstances attending it con-
ducing to show unfairness, before the
chancellor will refuse to confirm the sale .
Stump v .Martin ,9 Bush 285 .

alone sufficientto set aside a judicial
sale, but when the price bid is greatly
disproportioned to the actual value of
the property , only slight additional cir-
cumstances are required to justify and
make it the duty of the chancellor to set

connected with the sale from which
fraud may be presumed , or when the de-
fendant , by casualty or misfortune , has
been prevented from appearing in the

of his property ,it should be set aside.
Bean v .Hoffendorfer,84 Ky .685.

cient. Barnes v . Jackson ,85 Ky .407 .

otherwise directs, sale of real estate
should be made at court - house door .

classification.title: Bond of purchaser and effect of interest on.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the requirements for purchasers of property sold under court order. It details the necessity of giving a bond for the purchase price with approved surety, the interest rate on these bonds, and their legal force as judgments. It also specifies that no replevin will be allowed on executions issued upon these bonds, and sales will be for cash. The subsequent notes provide case law interpretations regarding the enforcement and challenges of these sales and bonds.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895 - Section 705
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines the requirements for pleadings in cases where the matter in controversy does not exceed fifty dollars. In such cases, pleadings can be oral and without verification. However, before a summons is issued, the plaintiff must file a written statement of the facts, account, or contract on which the action is based. The accompanying notes clarify that if the matter in controversy exceeds fifty dollars, pleadings must be in writing, and that interest due at the commencement of the action is considered part of the amount in controversy.

either party may appeal without refer-

Waters , 90 Ky .96 ; but the titleto land
is not involved where the only question
is as to priority between lien-holders.
Pittman v.Wakefield,90 Ky . 171.
(41 ) In action for trespass, where title
isbrought in issue, appeal lies, although
amount in controversy is less than one
hundred dollars. Thacker v .Crawford ,
5 R. 764 .
(42 ) Mandamus . Court of Appeals will
issuewrit of to compel circuit court to
grant an appeal to a party entitledto it.
Kelleyv. Toney ,95 Ky .338; Schmidt v.

return upon a summons , Wearen v .

refusingto qualify a person as deputy
