§ 238. (1) Reasonable doubt instruc-
tions. "A rationaldoubt is one growing
out of the evidence , and not a mere chi-
mera existing in the juror's mind ."
Graham v . Com ., 16 B. М. 587.
(2) In giving an instruction as to
reasonable doubt , " it seems to this court
that the better practice is to follow as
nearly as practicable the language of the
Criminal Code ” as expressed in this sec-
tion. Mickey v. Com ., 9 Bush 593 ;
Ward v . Com ., 14 Bush 233. The fol-
lowing instruction was approved : “ If
the jury , on allthe evidence , entertaina
reasonable doubt of the guilt of the de-
fendant , or of any fact necessary to
make out his guilt, they should acquit
him ; or if they find him guilty, and en-
tertain a reasonable doubt whether his
crime be murder or manslaughter , they
should find him guilty only of man-
slaughter." Brady v .Com .,11 Bush 282 ;
Payne v .Com ., 1 Met . 370.
(3) This section applies in allcriminal
prosecutions.Sowder v.Com .,8 Bush 432.
§ 239. Doubt as to degree of offense- in-
structions . " Whenever there is evidence
introduced which might be calculated to
raise a reasonable doubt of the degree of
the guilt of the accused ,the jury should
be instructed in pursuance of the pro-
vision of the Code just quoted " (sec.
239 ). Williams v . Com ., 80 Ky . 313.
§ 240. (1) Confessions. " Proof of con-
fessionsof a party in the presence of the
witness only, or of him and the adverse
party , although certainly competent
caution , because it is impossible for the
party to counteract it by other testi-
mony , because the expressions used are
easilymisunderstood or perverted,either
through mistake or design , and because
not the whole conversation, but only
parts of it, are generally detailed by the
witness . It isthe most dangerous species
of testimony held competent by the
law ." Becker v . Crow , 7 Bush 198.
(2) " It is a generalrule that confes-
sions, which are induced by hopes or
fears raised by the promise or threats of
the prosecutor or of any person having
authority over the prisoner at the time ,
are not considered voluntary , but as
having been made under mental duress ,
and therefore not competent ." Rector
v . Com ., 80 Ky .468. See Rutherford v.
Com ., 2 Met . 387 ; Young v . Com ., 8
Bush 366 ; Butler v . Com ., 2 Duv . 435 ;
Hudson v . Com ., 2 Duv . 531 ; but con-
fessionsprocured by deception are com-
petent . Wiggington v . Com ., 92 Ky .
282 .
(3) Although confessions improperly
obtained are not admissible,yet any facts
which have been brought to light in con-
sequence of such confessions may be
properly received in evidence ; as the
discovery of a hidden vial containing
poison, charged to have been adminis-
tered by the accused to the deceased.
Jane v . Com ., 2 Met . 30 ; Rector v .
Com .,80 Ky .468 .
(4 ) Whether the facts prove that a
confession has been extorted by duress
or not is a question of law for the court,

classification.title: Reasonable Doubt and Confessions
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines legal principles regarding reasonable doubt and the admissibility of confessions in criminal trials. It states that a defendant is entitled to acquittal if there is a reasonable doubt of their guilt, and should be convicted of a lesser offense if there is doubt about the degree of the crime. It also specifies that confessions made out of court must be corroborated by other evidence to warrant a conviction.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details general legal principles related to criminal procedure, specifically concerning reasonable doubt and the handling of confessions. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The language and principles are universally applicable to all defendants in criminal proceedings. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws.

§ 241[239]Testimonyof accomplicemust be corroborated. A conviction can
not be had upon the testimonyof an accomplice,unlesscorroborated
by other evidence tending to connect the defendantwith the com-
mission of the offense; and the corroboration is not sufficientif it
merely show thatthe offensewas committed,and the circumstances
thereof .

(5) " The whole of what the prisoner
said on the subject at the time of mak-
ing the confession should be taken to-
gether . If one part of a conversation is
relied on as proof of a confession of the
crime the prisoner has a right to lay be-
fore the court the whole of what was
said in that conversation ; not being con-
fined to so much only as is explanatory
of the part already proved against him ,
but being permitted to give evidence of
allthat was said upon that occasion rela-
tive to the subject matter in issue. If
the witness called to prove the confes-
sion of the prisoner says he does not re-
member allthe conversation, and that a
great many things were said in the con-
versation which he did not remember ,"
and he does not state that he remembers
the substance of all that was said , his
testimony should be excluded . Berry
v . Com ., 10 Bush 15. See Coffman v .
Com ., 10 Bush 495 .
(6) Statements of a woman in prosecu-
tion for detaining her , showing that she
was a willing party ,do not amount to a
confession. Cargill v . Com ., 93 Ky .
578 .
(7) " The manifest meaning of section
240 (238) is, that besides the proof of
any confession a defendant may have
made of his guilt, unless made in open
court ,there must , to warrant a convic-
tion, be other evidence conducing to
prove him guilty of the offense alleged,
or in other words , to show that such an
offense has been committed , and not
inconsistent with his guilt, and not
merely some other testimony ' which
might have no tendency whatever to
establish the charge ." Cunningham v .
Com ., 9 Bush 149, but in Wiggington v .
Com .,92 Ky . 282, this case is criticised
and it is held that it is sufficient cor-
roboration if there is other evidence
that such an offense was committed .
(8) Instructionin the language of this
sectionis proper . 92 Ky . 282, impliedly
overrulingCunningham v. Com ., 9 Bush
149 ; and Patterson v .Com .,86 Ky . 313.
§ 241. (1) Accomplice- evidence. An ac-
complice is one of several equally con-
cerned in the commission of a felony. A
conviction can not be had on the testi-
mony of an accomplice unless corrobo-
rated by other evidence ,and the corrobo-
ration must extend to every fact neces-
sary to establishthe fact that the offense
charged was committed , and that the
prisoner was the perpetrator. Miller v .
Com .,78 Ky .15.
(2 ) If two or more accomplices are
produced as witnesses they are not
deemed to corroborate each other . The
wife of an accomplice who has testified
against the accused may be introduced
as a witness to corroborate the testimony
of her husband . Blackburn v . Com .,
12 Bush 181 ; and see Smith v . Com ., 13
R. 369 .
(3) " The uncorroborated testimony of
an accomplice is wholly insufficientto
convict the accused of a crime , or of
any of its constituent elements , or to
render admissible any confessions or ad-
missions of the parties which depend
upon and must be preceded by evidence
of a conspiracy unless other evidence
besides that of the accomplice is pro-
duced tending to prove a conspiracy ,
and connect the accused therewith , and
evidence merely showing that the offense
was committed and the circumstances
thereof is as insufficientfor that pur-
pose as it would be to connect the ac-
cused with the commission of the of-
fenseitself." Bowling v .Com .,79 Ky .604.
(4 ) Under an indictment for murder
caused by an abortion a conviction may
be had on the statements of the deceased

classification.title: Testimony of accomplice must be corroborated.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the legal requirement that a conviction cannot be based solely on the testimony of an accomplice. Other evidence must be presented to corroborate the accomplice's testimony and connect the defendant to the commission of the offense. The corroboration must go beyond merely showing that the offense occurred and its circumstances.

The confidence is high because the text is clear and unambiguous regarding its legal purpose and lack of racial bias.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines the rules for keeping juries together during trials. It specifies when jurors must remain together (capital offenses) and when they may be permitted to separate (other felonies and misdemeanors), depending on the stage of the trial and the discretion of the court. It also details the oath required for officers in charge of the jury and the admonitions the court must give to the jury.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the procedural rules for jury conduct during trials in Kentucky in 1895. It focuses on the separation and management of juries, the duties of officers overseeing them, and court admonitions. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The rules apply generally to all defendants and jurors regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Verdict - How Rendered and General or Special Verdict
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines the procedures for rendering a jury's verdict. It details how a verdict is declared in court, the options for general or special verdicts, and the definitions of each. A general verdict upon a plea of guilty is 'guilty' and may include the punishment if the jury is required to determine it. Upon a plea of not guilty, the verdict is 'guilty' or 'not guilty,' specifying the offense and degree if applicable, and the punishment. It also covers verdicts upon pleas of former acquittal or conviction.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details the procedural aspects of jury verdicts in criminal and civil cases. It outlines how a verdict is delivered, the types of verdicts (general and special), and what constitutes each type. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

It provides examples related to assault, battery, breach of the peace, homicide, lottery, malicious stabbing, rape, robbery, and shooting.

classification.title: New Trial and Arrest of Judgment
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines procedures related to new trials and the arrest of judgment. It defines a new trial as the re-examination of an issue of fact by a new jury after a verdict has been given, and details the effect of granting a new trial, which places parties in the same position as if no trial had occurred. It also lists grounds for new trials and arrest of judgment, and specifies the timing and procedural requirements for motions related to these actions. Additionally, it includes provisions for handling verdicts in cases where the defense is insanity, including the possibility of committing the defendant to a lunatic asylum if found insane at the time of the verdict.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895, specifically dealing with legal procedures for new trials and arrest of judgment. It outlines the process for re-examining a case, the effects of a new trial, and grounds for arresting a judgment. It also addresses the specific scenario of an insanity defense. There is no mention of race or any provisions that would suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code. The confidence is high because the content is purely procedural and lacks any racial element. Human review is not needed.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895
classification.summary: This section of the 1895 Civil and Criminal Code outlines the grounds for granting a new trial in cases where a verdict has been rendered against the defendant and their substantial rights have been prejudiced. It lists seven specific circumstances, including the trial commencing and completing in the defendant's absence (for felonies), the jury receiving evidence out of court, the verdict being decided by lot, court misinstruction, the verdict being against law or evidence, the discovery of new favorable evidence, or general misconduct leading to an unfair trial. It also includes case law annotations related to these grounds, such as the requirement for the defendant to be present during felony trials and the waiver of errors if not raised at the time.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895, specifically detailing the grounds for a new trial. The statute itself is procedural and focuses on ensuring a fair trial by outlining conditions under which a verdict can be challenged. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The annotations also discuss general legal principles and procedural requirements without any racial undertones. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

It details the timelines for filing transcripts of records, the process for appeals initiated by the Commonwealth, and specifies that no summons or notice is required for appeals. It also states that a judgment of acquittal that bars future prosecution cannot be reversed, and lists errors that authorize the reversal of a judgment of conviction.

classification.title: Effect of appeal by defendant in penitentiary
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures and consequences for a defendant appealing a felony conviction that resulted in confinement in the penitentiary. It details how the defendant remains in the penitentiary during the appeal, what happens upon reversal (including removal back to county jail if a new trial is ordered), and how credit for time served is applied if the defendant is reconvicted after a new trial. It also addresses the process of bringing the defendant back from the penitentiary after a reversal.

classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 outlines the procedures for appeals to the Court of Appeals in misdemeanor cases. It details the process for affirming death penalty judgments, including the transmission of certificates to the governor for execution warrants. It also covers the jurisdiction of the Court of Appeals in penal actions and prosecutions for misdemeanors, as well as the time and manner of appeals by both the defendant and the Commonwealth. Specific sections address when execution is suspended, the necessity of summons, when judgments of acquittal cannot be reversed, errors that authorize reversal, attorney fees taxed as costs, and damages awarded on affirmance.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1895 and details the appellate process for misdemeanors and death penalty affirmations. It does not contain any language or provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code. The confidence is high because the content is purely procedural and legalistic, with no racial component.

classification.title: Appeals to Court of Appeals - Misdemeanors
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions under which a defendant can appeal a misdemeanor conviction in Kentucky. Appeals are only permitted if the judgment involves a fine exceeding fifty dollars, imprisonment exceeding thirty days, or if the judgment is for the defendant in cases where such penalties could have been imposed. It also details the time and manner of appealing, including the requirement to file the record with the Court of Appeals within sixty days and the conditions for suspending execution of the judgment.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the procedural aspects of appealing misdemeanor convictions in Kentucky. It sets financial and imprisonment thresholds for the right to appeal and outlines the process for filing an appeal. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or differentiate based on race. The statute appears to be a general procedural rule for the legal system. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Attorney's fees taxed as costs upon affirmance; Damages awarded on affirmance.
classification.summary: This section details the taxation of attorney's fees as costs upon the affirmance or reversal of a judgment in appeals. It specifies the amounts and division of these fees between the attorney-general and the attorney for the Commonwealth. It also outlines damages awarded at a rate of ten percent upon affirmance of a judgment for a fine, with a portion designated for the attorney-general. Amendments from 1880 are noted.

There is no mention of race, nor any indication of differential treatment based on race. The statute appears to be a general procedural rule within the legal code. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895 - Appeals to Circuit Courts
classification.summary: This section of the 1895 Civil and Criminal Code outlines the procedures and conditions for appeals to circuit courts in Kentucky. It details when appeals stand for trial, the timeframes for felony cases, and the judgment for costs. Specifically, Section 362 grants defendants the right to appeal to the circuit court if a judgment against them in a county judge's, justice's, city, or police court involves imprisonment or a fine of twenty dollars or more, unless otherwise specified by the statutes creating or regulating those courts.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the procedural aspects of appeals within the Kentucky court system. It outlines the timelines, jurisdiction, and conditions for appeals from lower courts to circuit courts. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute appears to be a general procedural law applicable to all defendants regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Appeals to Circuit Courts
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for a defendant to appeal a judgment from a county judge, justice's court, city court, or police court to the circuit court. It details the required documentation, the execution of covenants for costs and judgment performance, and the procedures for suspending or enforcing judgments during the appeal. The case is tried anew in the circuit court, and the judgment is affirmed if any amount is rendered against the defendant.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the procedural aspects of appealing a judgment from lower courts to the circuit court in Kentucky. It outlines the steps a defendant must take, including obtaining documentation, posting bond for costs and potential judgment enforcement, and the subsequent trial de novo in the circuit court. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute appears to be a general procedural law applicable to all defendants regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: SUPPRESSION OF RIOTS AND OF RESISTANCE TO LAWFUL AUTHORITY
classification.summary: This chapter outlines procedures for suppressing riots and resistance to lawful authority. It details how officers can command male inhabitants and military companies to assist in overcoming resistance, arresting resisters and their abettors, and punishing those who refuse to aid officers. It also addresses the Governor's authority to send aid if county power is insufficient and rules for military engagement.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the suppression of riots and resistance to lawful authority. It provides mechanisms for law enforcement to enlist assistance from citizens and the military to enforce the law. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The language is general and applies to all individuals who resist lawful authority. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Suppression of Riots - Resistance to Authority
classification.summary: This statute outlines procedures for suppressing riots and unlawful assemblies. It details how sheriffs, deputies, peace officers, and magistrates should command individuals to disperse, arrest those who refuse, and call upon citizens for aid. It also specifies when and how military forces can be called out to assist civil authorities in quelling violence and enforcing laws, and mandates that the military obey civil officers.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses the suppression of riots and unlawful assemblies, outlining the roles and responsibilities of civil and military authorities in maintaining order. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The language and scope of the statute are general and apply to all individuals involved in such disturbances. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is presented as part of a compiled legal code (Civil and Criminal Code 1895).

classification.title: Rules for government of military in suppression of riots
classification.summary: This statute outlines the rules for the deployment and use of military force in suppressing riots. It emphasizes a defensive posture, with troops only permitted to use lethal force under specific, escalating circumstances, such as direct attacks on soldiers or when rioters pose a clear danger to life or property and cannot be dispersed by less lethal means. It also mandates that magistrates or officers must first proclaim their authority and order the assembly to disperse before military action is taken.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the rules of engagement for military forces during riots. It details the conditions under which troops can use force, including firearms, to disperse rioters. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The focus is solely on maintaining public order and the use of force by the military in such situations. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It also covers the trial process, the duration for which security may be required, and proceedings for breaches of the bond. The text also includes case law references related to these provisions.

It outlines the process for requiring individuals to post a bond to ensure they do not commit offenses against others. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The procedures described are applicable to all individuals within the jurisdiction. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Jurisdiction of Courts - Criminal Jurisdiction of Justices and Police Courts
classification.summary: This statute outlines the criminal jurisdiction of Justices of the Peace and Police Courts in Kentucky. Justices of the Peace have exclusive jurisdiction in cases punishable by a fine not exceeding twenty dollars, and concurrent jurisdiction with circuit courts for cases with fines up to one hundred dollars or imprisonment up to fifty days. Police Courts in cities of various classes have original and exclusive jurisdiction over municipal ordinance violations and criminal jurisdiction similar to justices of the peace within their corporate limits. They also act as examining courts for felonies and misdemeanors.

[§ 2913, Ky. Stat.] **Fines and penalties—imprisonment—hard labor—custody of children.** Said court shall have power to impose such fines and penalties as may be prescribed by the Statutes of the State, or by the ordinances of the city, but no imprisonment exceeding thirty days shall be ordered, and no fine exceeding fifty dollars shall be imposed without the intervention of a jury, unless the right to have a jury is waived by the party to be tried. Cases in which the right to the custody and care of children is involved shall be tried by the court. When imprisonment is prescribed by the judge or jury trying the case, it shall be in the discretion of the judge or jury to direct whether or not the imprisonment shall be with hard labor, unless the statute imposing the penalty distinctly presents the exercise of such discretion. (*See further as to powers of court, Ky. Stat., secs. 2914-2921; appeals from, see Ky. Stat., sec. 2922.*)

---

CITIES OF THE SECOND CLASS.

[§ 3147, Ky. Stat.] **Jurisdiction of police court.** Said court shall have exclusive original jurisdiction in all prosecutions for the violation of the ordinances of the city, and jurisdiction, concurrent with the circuit court and justices of the peace, of all pleas of the Commonwealth arising within the limits of the city, except cases of felony; and said court shall have power and authority to take recognizances from persons charged with offenses recognizable before said court, to appear and answer the same as the circuit courts have, and a like power to enforce compliance with the same, and as to committing criminal offenders and sending them on for trial, said court shall have all power given by the general law to examining courts.

[§ 3148, Ky. Stat.] **Petit larceny and vagrancy—jurisdiction.** Said court shall have jurisdiction of all cases of petit larceny and vagrancy arising in said cities, respectively, and the justices of the peace are hereby required to make the recognizances of all persons charged with being vagrants or with petit larceny within said several cities returnable to the police court instead of the circuit court, and upon conviction of vagrancy or petit larceny, the person so convicted shall be sent to labor in the city prison or work-house for not less than three nor more than twelve months; presentment or indictment by a grand jury shall

It details where sessions should be held (cities of the second class and county seats), how cases should be docketed and tried based on defendant residency, and provisions for foreign corporations, non-residents, and common carriers. It also specifies requirements for official newspaper publications and jury selection when courts hold sessions in multiple locations within a county. The statute also allows for courts to certify rules to the Court of Appeals for judicial notice.

entries.ocr_text:
564 COURTS HAVING FOUR JUDGES. [APPENDIX

§ 1022. **Commission of judges.** Each of said judges shall be commissioned as a circuit judge for the district for which he is elected, and shall have all the powers of a circuit judge.

§ 1023. **Criminal, chancery, common pleas and law and equity branches.** Every circuit court in the Commonwealth having four judges shall have four branches, to be named, respectively, criminal, chancery, common pleas and law and equity branch.

§ 1024. **Continuous session in each court.** Every such court shall have continuous session.

§ 1025. **Jurisdiction how divided—assignment of causes—duty of clerk.** Of cases within the jurisdiction of such court, all criminal prosecutions and proceedings on bail bonds and recognizance in criminal cases shall be brought and prosecuted in the criminal branch; litigation prosecuted in the other branches shall be divided between them, according to rules of court, to be made in general term. Until such rules are made, whenever eighteen equitable actions or special proceedings of such litigation are unassigned, the clerk shall, in the presence of one of the four judges, write the style or number of each of said causes upon a separate card or slip, and place the said eighteen cards or slips in a box or receptacle therefor prepared, and draw them indiscriminately therefrom, one at a time, and assign them to said branches, giving the chancery branch the first twelve drawn, to the common pleas branch the next three drawn, and to the law and equity branch the remaining three; and whenever eighteen actions or special proceedings, not equitable, are unassigned, said clerk shall, in like manner, draw, in the presence of one of the four judges, and assign them to the last two named branches, giving to the common pleas branch the first nine drawn, and to the law and equity branch the last nine. Until said causes or proceedings are so assigned any orders required therein may be made therein by any one of said four judges. None but equitable actions and special proceedings shall be assigned to the chancery branch. No rule of court shall assign more law or equity causes to one or the other of the two branches, common pleas and law and equity.

§ 1026. **Assignment of judges—vacancies.** Each judge of said court shall be assigned to preside over one branch of said court during his entire term of office, except as hereinafter provided. Said assignment shall be made as follows: The judge elected by ballots marked criminal division shall, during his entire term, preside over the criminal branch; the judge elected by ballots marked chancery division shall, during his entire term, preside over the chancery branch; the judge

classification.title: Special Judges of Circuit Court

It also specifies the requirements for an affidavit to disqualify a judge and the timing for objections.

The document type is 'codes' as it appears to be part of a compiled legal code from 1895.

Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It also specifies conditions under which a change of venue can be demanded in justice and police courts, and provisions for when a justice is unable to attend or try a case.

ARTICLE 3 .
IN CRIMINAL CASES .
§ 1109.Applicationofdefendantor Commonwealth- adjacentcounty. When
a criminalor penal prosecutionis pending in any circuitcourt,the
judge thereofshall, upon the applicationof the defendantor the
Commonwealth , orderthe trialto be had in some otheradjacent
countyto which thereisno validobjection,ifitappearsthatthe
defendant or the Commonwealth can not have a fair trial in the
countywheretheprosecutionispending; and ifthejudgeissatisfied
thata fairtrialcannot be had in an adjacentcounty,he may order
the trialto be had in the most convenientcounty in which a fair
trial can be had .
§ 1109.(1) Adjoiningcounty- objection.
Where , upon motion of the defendant
and by consentof the attorneyfor the
State, a change of venue is had to a
county not adjoining the one in which
the offense was committed , it istoo late,
after trial and conviction , for the defend-
ant to object to the jurisdictionof the
court. Lightfootv. Com ., 80 Ky . 516 ;
Hourigan v.Com ., 94 Ky .520.
(2) Upon an applicationforchange of
venue , when all the adjoining counties
in the districthave been objected to, it
is proper to send the case to an adjoin-
ing county in another district. An error
in deciding as to what county the case is
to be sent does not invalidate the pro-
ceedings. Kennedy v. Com .,78 Ky .447.
(3) Where objectionis made to the
county where the court proposes to send
the case,the objectionmust be a valid
one,and the court may hear proof on
this point,both upon the part of the ac-
cused and the Commonwealth . Mickey
v . Com .,13 Bush 237.
(4 ) Burden upon applicantto make out
case. To entitlethe defendant to a change
of venue ,the onus is on him to at least
make out a prima faciecase. The mere
filingof a petitionwithout affidavitsor
oral testimony to support itis not suffi-
cient. Wilkerson v . Com ., 88 Ky . 29 ;
Hasson v . Com ., 10 R. 1054. Weight to
be given to the judgment refusing the
change. Dilgerv.Com .,Id.,550. When
the evidence shows that there are rea-
sonable grounds to believe that the de-
fendant can not have a fair trial in the
county where the offense is committed ,
it is the duty of the court to grant the
change . Johnson v . Com ., 82 Ky . 116.
(5) Discretionof court. The trialcourt
has a sound discretion in determining
whether or not a motion for a change of
venue should be sustained, and its con-
clusions will not be disturbed unless pal-
pably abused . Howard v . Com ., 15 R.
873; Wren v.Com .,8 R. 418; Hasson v.
Com ., 10 R. 1054 .
(6) Jurisdictionalfacts. The statements
contained in the affidavitshowing the
necessity for a change of venue are not
jurisdictionalfacts. Lightfoot v .Com .,
80 Ky .516; Smith v.Com .,95 Ky .322.
(7 ) New indictment. Where a case is
transferred by Commonwealth ,the court
of county from which transfer is made
loses all jurisdiction over case, and an
indictment found in that county against
defendant for same offense is a nullity,
although indictment transferred has
been filedaway . Smith v .Com ., 95 Ky .
322 .
(8) Rightof Commonwealth . The Legis-
lature may , by a general law , provide
for a change of venue in criminal cases
upon motion of the Commonwealth as
well as upon motion of the defendant .

classification.title: Change of Venue in Criminal Cases
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures and conditions under which a change of venue can be granted in criminal or penal prosecutions in Kentucky circuit courts. It allows for the trial to be moved to an adjacent county or, if necessary, to the most convenient county where a fair trial can be had, upon application by either the defendant or the Commonwealth. It also details various legal precedents and considerations regarding objections to venue changes, the burden of proof on the applicant, and the discretion of the court.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses the legal process for changing the location of a criminal trial. It focuses on ensuring a fair trial by allowing for venue changes when impartiality is compromised in the original county. There is no mention of race, and the provisions apply equally to both the defendant and the Commonwealth. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it appears to be part of a compiled legal code from 1895.

Additionally, it addresses the process for representatives of a deceased party to appeal or bring a writ of error.

of the United States and to regulate the removal of causes from State courts, and for other purposes," and section six hundred and forty of the Revised Statutes, and all laws and parts of laws in conflict with the provisions of this act, be and the same are hereby repealed:

*Provided*, That this act shall not affect the jurisdiction over or disposition of any suit removed from the court of any State, or suit commenced in any court of the United States, before the passage hereof, except as otherwise expressly provided in this act.

---

## CHAPTER VII.

### RULES OF COURT OF APPEALS, ADOPTED JANUARY, 1895.

The following were ordered to be recorded as rules of practice of the court:

1.  **Division of court into two departments—sessions—chief justice's powers and duties.** In accordance with section 118 of the Constitution, this court, after January 1, 1895, will be divided into two departments, each one of which shall consist of three judges, besides the chief justice, who shall preside over each department. Each division shall sit on alternate days during each week, when not in joint session, to hear arguments and motions and deliver opinions. Opinions shall be delivered as the judgment of the court without reference to the department delivering them. When the chief justice is absent, or, if present, from any cause fails to preside, the judge next oldest in commission shall preside with each department, and shall require the presence of a judge from either department when necessary to constitute a majority of the entire body. The cases, when submitted, shall be assigned by the chief justice to each department, and in such a manner as to equalize the burden.

2.  **Full court to hear and decide cases—when.** Whenever a case involves a constitutional question, either Federal or State, or in any case where, in the opinion of the chief justice, the importance of the case requires, both departments shall hear the argument, whether oral or written, and pass on the questions involved; and in cases where the judges composing one department do not concur, it shall be the duty of the chief justice to notify the other department, and have the questions at issue disposed of in joint session.

classification.title: Civil Code Forms - Indemnifying Bond
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code provides a form for an indemnifying bond. It outlines the process where a sheriff or constable, after levying an execution on property, demands an indemnity bond from the plaintiff if there are potential claimants to the property. The bond is conditioned on the obligors paying damages to any claimant whose property is seized or sold. The text details the petition a claimant would file to recover damages if the conditions of the bond are broken.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1895 - Distress Warrants, Bonds Taken Under
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code outlines the procedures and forms for distress warrants, which are legal instruments used by landlords to seize a tenant's personal property to satisfy unpaid rent. It includes the form for the distress warrant itself, a replevin bond that a tenant can give to regain possession of the property, and references to forms for bonds of indemnity to officers and bonds to suspend sale under a distress warrant.

classification.title: Form of Bill of Exceptions and Rules
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code of 1895 outlines the procedural requirements for creating a bill of exceptions in legal proceedings. It details what information needs to be included, such as witness testimonies, court instructions, objections, and rulings, and clarifies what can be omitted if it is already recorded in the order book. The purpose is to document facts that occur during a trial that would not otherwise be preserved.
classification.reasoning: The provided text describes the procedural rules for creating a 'bill of exceptions' in civil court cases. This is a legal document used to record specific events or rulings during a trial that are not automatically part of the official court record. The text focuses entirely on the mechanics of legal documentation and does not contain any language, provisions, or implications related to racial segregation, discrimination, or any other aspect of Jim Crow laws. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow statute.

646 CIVIL CODE FORMS - FORM OF BILL OF EXCEPTIONS .
The followingruleswillbe of servicetotheyoung practitioner:
Rule1. If a reversalis asked because the evidence does not supportthe ver-
dict then the whole of the evidence of each witness must appear in the bill, and
in place of stating that " the witness testifiedin substance as follows," state at
the conclusion of the testimony of each witness " the above is the testimony in full
of the witness ."
Rule2. If the adverse party asks a witnessa questionthat isobjected to, the
billshould statethe question objectedto, and if the objectionis overruledand
the witness allowed to answer question the billshould statethe answer and that " an
exception was taken to the ruling of the court ;" the following statement will illus-
tratethe practice: The plaintiffasked witness[here statequestion], defendant
objected, his objection was overruled and witness permitted to answer the question
[stateanswer] to which rulingof the courtdefendantexcepted. The objectionand
exception should be made at the time and the billshould so show .
Rule 3. If a party asks his witness a question and the adverse party objects,and
his objection issustained, the party should then write out and submit to the court
the answer he expects the witness to make to the question ; and if the court declines
to permit witness to answer , the billshould state the question and the answer and
should show that an exception was taken at the time to the ruling of the court in
excluding answer .
Rule 4. When adverse party offersany evidence or to take any step during trial
that is objected to, it is necessary that the bill should show that an objectionwas
taken at the time , and also that an exceptionwas taken at the time to the ruling of
the court in overruling the objection.
Rule5. When a party offersto take any step or introduceany evidencedur-
ing trial that an objection is made and sustained to, an exceptionshould be taken
to the ruling of the court at the time , and the billshould so show .
Rule6. If deeds,papers,depositions,books, recordsor other like evidenceis
offered,and an objection sustained to itsintroduction, it should be copied in the bill
and identifiedas follows: " Plaintiffhere offeredto read a deed [or whatever itmay
be ,describing it] to which defendant objected, his objection was sustained and said
deed not allowed to be introduced as evidence , to which ruling of the court the
plaintiff at the time excepted . Said deed is as follows: (here insert it], or- the
defendanthere offeredto read a letter[or whatever it may be ,describingit] from
Frank Hall to Wm . Burton , dated January 1, 1895 , plaintiffobjected to the reading
of said letter,his objection was overruled and said letterpermitted to be read as evi-
dence , to which ruling of the court plaintiffat the time excepted . Said letterisas
follows[here insertit].
Rule7. If the court,on itsown motion,rulesagainsta party,an exceptiontaken
by him at the time tothe rulingis sufficient; an objectionisnot required.
Rule 8. The billshould contain each instruction offered and refused and given,
and should show that an objectionwas made at the time to givingthoseofferedby
the adverse party , and if they were given , that an exception was taken at the time.
If the court on itsown motion gives an instruction, an exception to it will be suf-
ficient, and if a party offers an instruction which is refused, the bill should
show that an exception to the ruling was taken at the time . A general exception
toallthe instructionshas been held sufficient,but the saferplan is to objectand
except [or except , as the case may be]to the ruling of the court upon each instruction.
Rule9. Itisnot necessarythatthe attorneyinpreparingthe billshouldcopy into
it alldeeds , papers and exhibits offered or used , or the instructions, but he should
arrangethe billso that each willbe identified, and so that the clerkin copying the

classification.title: CAPIAS PRO FINE
classification.summary: This section of the 1895 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the legal forms and procedures for issuing a 'Capias Pro Fine,' which is an order for the arrest of an individual to satisfy a monetary fine and costs owed to the Commonwealth. It details the process for executions upon judgments from both Circuit Courts and Justices of the Peace, specifying the commands to sheriffs and constables to take the body of the defendant and keep them until the fine and costs are satisfied. It also includes provisions for execution against property and on replevin bonds.
classification.reasoning: The provided text describes legal procedures for enforcing monetary fines and costs in criminal cases. It outlines the mechanisms for arrest and detention until payment is made. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or differentiate based on race. The language is procedural and applies generally to any individual convicted and fined. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws. Confidence is high because the text is purely procedural and lacks any racial component.
